1:
The chapter opens on a battlefield, and the witnesses agree that the renewed Philistine invasion is no accident of timing. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the ground. Keil and Delitzsch locate Shochoh at the present Shuweikeh in the Wady Sumt, some three and a half hours south-west of Jerusalem, and Ephes-dammim in the ruins of Damm, in the hilly border country between the mountains of Judah and the plain of Philistia (see at Joshua 15:35). Ellicott reckons the locality twelve or fifteen miles south-west of Jerusalem and nine or ten from Bethlehem, and hears something ominous in the very name Ephes-dammim, the "boundary of blood," which tells of the constant border warfare of that neighbourhood. Clarke, following Calmet, dates the campaign about eight years after David's anointing, and reminds us that there was war with the Philistines all Saul's days (1 Samuel 14:52). Ellicott adds the literary note that the compiler is henceforth mainly concerned with the story of David and how he "gradually rose in popular estimation," the book being drawn from documents of the time stored up in the prophetic schools.

On why the Philistines struck now, the schools converge. The Reformed Gill supposes they had heard of Saul's melancholy and distraction and thought it a proper opportunity to avenge their last slaughter. The Wesleyan Wesley compresses the point: probably they had learned that Samuel had forsaken Saul and that the king himself was now unfit for business. The Puritan Matthew Henry turns it into a lesson on the church's security. Israel's ground would never have been footing for Philistine armies had Israel been faithful to their God; the enemies of the church are watchful to take all advantages, and they never have greater advantage than when her protectors have provoked God's Spirit and prophets to leave them.

Henry alone lingers on the mercy hidden in the moment: the evil spirit had for the present left Saul (1 Samuel 16:23), for "business is a good antidote against melancholy," and God in mercy to Israel suspended the judgment while the nation's affairs hung in the balance. The voices do not divide; together they read the gathering armies as at once a real historical campaign on a well-known frontier and a providential stage. Henry draws the thread that will run through the whole chapter: the Philistines meant to retrieve the credit they had lately lost, but God "designed hereby to ripen him for destruction and to make Israel's deliverance the more illustrious." The border of blood is being made ready for a deliverance that no one in either camp yet foresees.
2:
The witnesses turn from the invaders to the defenders, and the grammatical-historical commentators do the topography. Keil and Delitzsch render the site the terebinth valley, Emek ha-Elah, a broad plain by the Wady Musur, and mark carefully the deeper cutting the brook had made through it: the two hosts stood opposed on the slopes, "and the valley" — the sharper ravine — "was between them." Clarke notes that some translate Elah "the turpentine valley," or the valley of the terebinth trees, and others the valley of oaks, the name pointing to the great tree that gave the plain its landmark. The scene is thus not a level field for a pitched engagement but a defile in which each army could hold an almost impregnable position, a fact that will shortly make single combat the natural way of deciding the campaign.

The Reformed Gill keeps his eye on the king. Saul, "being cured, at least being better of his disorder, through the music of David," and roused and alarmed by the Philistine invasion — which might itself serve to dissipate any remains of his distemper, or to prevent its return — got his forces together and faced the enemy. Gill thus quietly ties the muster back to the harp of the previous chapter (1 Samuel 16:23): the shepherd's music had steadied the king enough to take the field, though the Spirit that once made him daring had departed.

There is no debate here; the interest is descriptive and scene-setting. Yet the witnesses let the geography carry a theological weight. The armies of Israel and the armies of the Philistines are drawn up "in array," the whole apparatus of ancient war assembled, and between them lies a valley that will hold, before the forty days are out, only two combatants. The commentators are content to lay out the ground plainly, knowing that the very ordinariness of the encampment — a king mustering his men on the hills of Judah — is the setting into which an extraordinary providence is about to break. The valley of the terebinth becomes, in their hands, less a military map-reference than the appointed place where the living God will show that he saves not with sword and spear but by the weakest of instruments.
3:
This verse fixes the deadlock, and the witnesses attend chiefly to a small apparent difficulty in the geography. The Reformed Gill notices that the previous verse placed Israel encamped "by the valley," while this one sets them upon a mountain facing the Philistines on the opposite height, and he reports two rabbinic reconciliations. Kimchi supposed that the grand army lay encamped in the valley, while the first ranks — "the first battalion" — ascended the mountain to face the enemy; Vatablus took it to be one and the same range, the Philistines forming their front on one part of it and the Israelites pitching on another, with the rest of each army spread in the valley below. Either way, the picture is of two forces posted on rising ground with a ravine between.

The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the terms. Keil and Delitzsch distinguish the broad plain from the narrower gully, taking the "valley" here (ha-gai) as the deeper cutting the brook had made in the level ground, so that the armies stood on the two flanking slopes while the watercourse ran between them. Clarke reads it simply and practically: these were "two eminences or hills, from which they could see and talk with each other" — a detail that matters, since the whole drama of the challenge depends on a champion in the valley being able to make himself heard by the ranks on the heights.

The voices do not contend; they agree that the two armies faced one another across a defended natural barrier that neither cared to cross. The stalemate is itself part of the design. Each position being well-nigh impregnable, as the commentators repeatedly observe, a general engagement was unattractive to both sides, and the stage was set for the Philistines to force the issue by their giant. The topography that keeps the hosts apart is precisely what gives Goliath his opening; the valley that divides Israel from Philistia will, in a few days, be the arena in which a shepherd's sling decides what the massed battalions on the hills dared not attempt. The witnesses lay out the ground with a plain eye, content to let the ravine between the mountains do its quiet work in the narrative that follows.
4:
The champion steps forward, and the witnesses divide their labour between his strange title and his prodigious size. On the title, the grammatical-historical and Reformed voices agree in substance. Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew ish habbenayim "the middle-man, who decides a war between two armies by a single combat," noting that Luther guessed "the giant" from the Septuagint's "mighty man," though the Greek elsewhere in the chapter renders it more correctly. Gill gives the same range — "a middle person, or a man between two" — either one who stood between the two armies or a "dueller." Clarke surveys the versions, which "know not well what to make of this man": the Vulgate calls him a bastard, the Septuagint "a strong man," the Targum "a man from between them," the Arabic and Syriac "a gigantic man," and Josephus "an immensely great man"; Clarke settles on our "champion," from campus, the field, as the aptest rendering — one who fights in the field to settle a dispute between nations.

On the height — six cubits and a span — the same school weighs the arithmetic, and here a genuine textual difference surfaces. Clarke lays out the whole calculation: taking the cubit at various estimates, Goliath stands anywhere from nine feet nine inches to, by Bishop Cumberland's exact tables, eleven feet ten inches, though Clarke himself judges "nine feet nine inches the most reasonable." He observes candidly that the versions do not agree, for the Septuagint and Josephus read "four cubits and a span," while the Codex Alexandrinus keeps the Hebrew. Wesley settles for "at least nine feet nine inches high," adding that this is not strange, since Herodotus, Diodorus Siculus, and Pliny mention men seven cubits tall. Keil and Delitzsch, following Thenius, make it about nine feet two inches — "a great height, no doubt, though not altogether unparalleled."

The debate over the exact stature is real but friendly, turning on the length of an ancient cubit and the reliability of the Greek text against the Hebrew. On the essential point the voices are one. Goliath is of the Anakim who kept their ground in Gath in Joshua's day (compare Joshua 11:22), a monster of a man whose bulk is meant to make the coming victory the more plainly the Lord's. Whether he stood nine feet or eleven, the witnesses agree he was formidable enough that all Israel trembled — and small enough, in the end, for a single smooth stone.
5:
The narrator begins the famous inventory of Goliath's armour, and the witnesses read it as a study in terror by artifice — "art, as well as nature, made him terrible," in Matthew Henry's phrase from the larger passage. On the coat of mail the grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices agree closely. Ellicott renders it "breastplate of scales," and thinks it made not of chain but of metal scales like those of a fish, "whose defensive coat was no doubt imitated at a very early date by this warlike race who dwelt on the sea-shore." Clarke gives the same picture from the Hebrew shiryon kaskassim, "a coat of mail formed of plates of brass overlapping each other, like the scales of a fish, or tiles of a house," such as may still be seen in old armouries. Wesley and Gill concur: brass plates laid over one another like a fish's scales, the helmet perhaps of leather covered with metal, or of solid brass.

The one point of open difference concerns the weight. The text gives five thousand shekels of brass, and the commentators disagree because they reckon the shekel differently. Clarke, following Bishop Cumberland's tables and rating the shekel at two hundred and nineteen grains, computes the coat at exactly one hundred and fifty-six pounds four ounces avoirdupois — "a vast weight for a coat of mail, but not all out of proportion to the man." Wesley, taking the common shekel at a fourth of an ounce, arrives at seventy-eight pounds, "a weight not unsuitable to a man of such vast strength." Ellicott declines the precise figure but observes that "the weight of the different pieces of the giant's panoply largely exceeds the weight of mediaeval suits of armour."

The disagreement is only over the standard of the shekel, not over the point the weight is meant to make. Every witness treats the armour as evidence of overwhelming natural advantage — the helmet of brass, the scaled corselet flexible over back and sides, the sheer mass of metal that no ordinary man could bear or oppose. The effect of the inventory, the commentators sense, is to pile up the odds against Israel so that the reader feels the hopelessness the ranks felt, and so that the deliverance, when it comes by a sling and a stone, will be seen to owe nothing to metal and everything to the God of the armies of Israel.
6:
The inventory continues with the greaves and the piece of armour slung between Goliath's shoulders, and the witnesses read on with the same eye for detail. On the greaves the Reformed Gill and the Wesleyan Clarke agree: these were "a sort of boots, or leg harnesses," of brass, covering the shin from knee to instep. Gill, borrowing from Homer's description of the greaves of Iolaus and the Greeks, reckons them at some thirty pounds — double the weight of the helmet. Clarke adds from the ancient monuments that such greaves were "commonly worn only on one leg," and cites Vegetius that the Roman foot-soldiers called Scutati "were obliged to use iron greaves on their right legs," the leg most exposed in the fighting posture.

The one small crux is the brazen object "between his shoulders." The grammatical-historical commentators canvass the possibilities without forcing a verdict. Keil and Delitzsch take the word kidon to signify "a lance, or small spear," while noting that the Septuagint, Vulgate, and Luther render it a brazen shield; Thenius would even emend the text to read a small shield slung over the back, since "between his shoulders" seems awkward for a spear. But Keil answers the difficulty from Bochart, who gathered examples from Homer and Virgil to show that the ancients carried their swords slung over the shoulders, so no emendation is needed. Clarke lists the same range of opinions — a covering for the shoulders, a javelin, a lance, a club, a sword — and settles the matter partly by observing that the kidon is distinguished from the shield in v.41 and is elsewhere translated a spear (as at Joshua 8:18).

The debate is narrow and technical, and the witnesses hold it lightly; whether the piece between the shoulders was a small shield or a slung javelin does not change the impression the passage builds. The commentators let the greaves and the brazen weapon add to the mounting sense of a man armoured at every point, from head to feet, so that no natural weakness seems left for an assailant to reach. That the whole panoply will presently be defeated by an unarmoured boy is the irony the inventory is quietly preparing — the more brass, the greater the fall.
7:
The description of Goliath's arms reaches its climax in the spear, and the witnesses vie in conveying its enormity. The Reformed Gill reckons that, in proportion to the giant's stature, the spear must have been some twenty-six feet long, "since Hector's in Homer was eleven cubits, or sixteen feet and a half." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the text and the metal: the shaft was like a weaver's beam, and the iron point weighed six hundred shekels, "about seventeen pounds"; they note the Keri and the parallels at 2 Samuel 21:19 and 1 Chronicles 20:5, where the word should be read "wood," that is, shaft, and observe that the bearer of the great shield (the zinnah) went before him.

The Wesleyan Clarke adds the running total. Taking the proportions of the several pieces, he estimates the whole armour of Goliath at "not less than two hundred and seventy-two pounds thirteen ounces," and sets it against Plutarch's testimony that an ordinary soldier's complete panoply weighed one talent, or sixty pounds, while one Alcimus in the army of Demetrius was reckoned a prodigy because his armour weighed two talents, or a hundred and twenty. Wesley, not to be outdone, protests that the whole is "not so much by far as one Athanatus did manage, of whom Pliny relates that he saw him come into the theatre with arms weighing twelve thousand ounces," and takes the shield to have been carried before Goliath "probably for state, for he that was clad in brass little needed a shield."

The witnesses do not disagree on anything of weight; they are heaping up superlatives to a single end. The spear's head is a small ploughshare of iron, the shaft a weaver's beam, and a whole man goes before him merely to carry his shield. Behind the antiquarian comparisons to Homer, Plutarch, and Pliny lies a shared theological instinct: the passage is measuring, pound by pound, the impossibility that any Israelite could match this man on his own terms. The reader is meant to feel the full crushing weight of the Philistine's advantage, so that when it is undone by five smooth stones the glory will fall wholly to the Lord who "saveth not with sword and spear."
8:
Goliath now speaks, and the witnesses fasten on the pride and the taunt of his challenge. The grammatical-historical commentators catch the emphasis the English blurs. Ellicott insists the literal force is "Am not I the Philistine?" — the famous warrior "whom you know too well" — and hears in "ye servants to Saul" a deliberate jibe at the memory of the king's former glory: will none of the famous servants of the warrior-king dare to meet him? Keil and Delitzsch paraphrase the whole: "I am the man who represents the strength of the Philistines, and ye are only servants of Saul; if ye have heroes, choose one out, that we may decide the matter in single combat."

Both these voices, with the Wesleyan Clarke, report the expansion the Targum of Jonathan adds to the giant's boast. There Goliath claims to be the very man who slew Eli's sons Hophni and Phinehas and carried the ark of the covenant captive into the temple of Dagon, and who has always gone at the head of the army, yet has never been granted so much as the command of a thousand — whereas Saul was made king for his services. Clarke gives the speech at length; Ellicott judges that this Targum, "although comparatively a late compilation, doubtless embodied many ancient national traditions." The Reformed Gill keeps to the plain scene: the giant stood in the valley and cried with a voice as strong as his frame, so that his words rose to the battalions posted on the hill and were the more easily heard.

Ellicott presses the sharpest question the verse raises. Must we not deem it probable, he asks, that the news of the separation of the prophet from the king had reached Philistia, and that the present daring challenge was owing to their knowledge that the Spirit of the Lord — "whom we know these enemies of the Hebrews dreaded with so awful a dread" — had departed from Saul and his armies? On this reading the taunt is not mere bravado but a calculated insolence, timed to a moment when Israel's king is known to be forsaken. The witnesses agree that the challenge is at bottom a defiance not of Saul's servants only but of the covenant God behind them, and it is exactly this that will rouse a shepherd where it silences an army.
9:
Goliath sets his terms — the losing nation to serve the winner — and the witnesses read them with a shared scepticism about how binding such a bargain really was. The Reformed Gill observes bluntly that the giant had "neither commission nor authority" to pledge his people to servitude, "nor did the Philistines think themselves obliged to abide by what he said, since, when he was slain, they did not yield themselves servants to the Israelites." The Wesleyan Clarke says the same in a sentence: "Of this stipulation we hear nothing farther." The proposal, in other words, was a boast dressed as a treaty; the event would show that a beaten Philistia fled rather than surrendered.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the challenge in its historical and literary world. Keil and Delitzsch give the plain sense of the offer and pass to the mockery of the next verse, where Goliath declares he has "defied the ranks of Israel this day" and demands a man to fight. Ellicott supplies the wider frame: each army held a position well-nigh impregnable, so that a single combat seemed the only way of deciding the campaign; and "in those far back times such single combats between renowned chieftains of the opposing armies were not by any means uncommon." He points the reader to the Iliad, whose colloquies before the deadly duel are "not altogether unlike the haughty, boastful challenge of the giant Philistine" — recalling how Glaucus bids Diomede "come hither, that you may quickly reach the goal of death."

There is no real division among the witnesses here. They agree that the terms were, on their face, a reasonable way to spare two armies the hazard of a general engagement, and that the practice had ancient precedent. They agree too that the offer was never honoured, and could not bind a nation to the vaunt of one man. What the commentators quietly underline is the contrast between the confidence with which Goliath disposes of Israel's future and the emptiness of his authority to do so. He speaks as though the outcome were already his, staking the freedom of a people on his own arm; and the narrative will let that arrogance run its full length before the God whom he has not reckoned with overturns the whole wager with a stone. The reader is meant to remember, when the Philistines break and run, that the giant's grand condition was worth exactly nothing.
10:
Goliath's challenge reaches its most defiant pitch, and the witnesses concentrate on the word rendered "I defy." The Reformed Gill glosses it "I reproach" — should Israel not send down a man, he would upbraid them with cowardice and disdain them as a people without a single champion. The Wesleyan Clarke presses the Hebrew harder: ani cheraphti means "I strip and make bare the armies of Israel," a vaunt of contempt for a foe none of whom dared to fight him. Clarke then sets the taunt in its cultural context, noting from the Dhunoor Veda Shastra that among the Hindoos it was common, before an engagement, to challenge the enemy with terms of abuse "similar to those used by Goliath," and adding that we find the same in Homer, whose heroes "scold each other heartily before they begin to fight."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the defiance as calculated mockery: the insult lay in Goliath's designating the Israelites as mere "servants of Saul" and in the whole triumphant tone with which he issued his summons — "give me a man, that we may fight together." He has, in his own words, "mocked the ranks of Israel this day," and no one steps forward to answer. The commentators let the giant's scorn stand at its full height, unrebuked by any human voice.

Beneath the philology the witnesses hear one thing that Goliath does not: whom he is really defying. The armies he strips and shames are, as the next scenes will insist, "the armies of the living God," and the reproach he flings across the valley falls not on Saul's servants only but on the covenant Lord whose people they are. The commentators note the daily, wearisome repetition of this defiance — morning and evening for forty days — as the measure both of the giant's swelling pride and of Israel's deepening paralysis. The stage is now fully set: a boasting champion who reckons only with flesh, and a silent army that has forgotten its God. Into that silence the challenge hangs unanswered, ripening the Philistine for the destruction that his own tongue is inviting, until a shepherd who has not forgotten the living God comes down to the valley.
11:
The challenge lands, and the witnesses read Israel's terror as the visible fruit of a king from whom the Spirit has departed. The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the contrast with former days. Saul the king was perhaps "restrained from personally accepting the challenge by motives of dignity," but his marked silence and the utter hopelessness of his army "reads in strange contrast to the former records of Hebrew daring." Where, Ellicott asks, was Jonathan, "ever the bravest of the brave," and his gallant armour-bearer? He notes what is conspicuously absent: "no inquiry of the Urim and Thummim, no mention of prayer to the God of the armies of Israel. An evil spirit was indeed upon the King of Israel." Clarke states the plain cause: "they saw no man able to accept the challenge."

The Wesleyan Wesley finds the dismay strange, "considering the glorious promises and their late experience of divine assistance," and repeats the question about Jonathan, who in the last war had so bravely engaged a whole army of the Philistines (compare 1 Samuel 14:52). His answer is at once providential and humbling: "Doubtless he did not feel himself so stirred up of God as he did at that time. As the best, so the bravest of men, are no more than what God makes them. Jonathan must sit still now, because this honour is reserved for David." The Puritan Matthew Henry traces the panic to its head: "The people would not have been dismayed but that they observed Saul's courage failed him; and it is not to be expected that, if the leader be a coward, the followers should be bold." When the Spirit of the Lord had once come upon Saul (as at 1 Samuel 11:6), none was more daring; now that the Spirit had departed, "even the big looks and big words of a single Philistine make him change colour."

The voices do not divide; they read the fear of a whole army as the shadow of one man's lost anointing. Together they draw the lesson that valour is not a native possession but a gift, granted and withdrawn at God's pleasure — Jonathan silent because unstirred, Saul blanched because forsaken, the ranks trembling because their leader trembles. The stage is thus emptied of every human champion, so that the deliverance reserved for David may be seen to come, when it comes, from the God whom the terrified camp has forgotten to consult.
12:
With David reintroduced, the witnesses confront both a portrait of humility and a famous textual difficulty. The Puritan Matthew Henry dwells on the grace of the young man's obscurity. Having tasted the honour of a courtier and holding a claim on Saul's household, David "went home to Bethlehem and returned to keep his father's sheep; this was a rare instance, in a young man that stood so fair for preferment, of humility and affection to his parents. He knew better than most how to come down again after he had begun to rise." Henry reads Jesse's great age as an honour — "his hoary head was a crown of glory to him" — and suspects the three elder brothers, envying David's place at court, had a hand in sending him home while they marched to the war.

The grammatical-historical commentators face the harder question: why the Septuagint omits this verse and all that follows to v.31, along with vv.55-58. Ellicott judges the omission was due to the difficulty of David's apparent first introduction here, the translators "not un-frequently adding or subtracting from the text when anything met them which they could not readily understand"; the passage, he holds, is "undoubtedly genuine," and is best read (with the Syriac) as a parenthesis down to v.14. On the odd phrase "went among men for an old man," Ellicott follows Jerome's Vulgate, while noting that Keil renders it "among the weak" — Jesse "reckoned among the aged." Wesley takes it simply: Jesse was an old man, "therefore he went not himself to the camp."

Here the Wesleyan Clarke becomes the chapter's chief sceptic. He reports that the twelfth through thirty-first verses, the forty-first, and the close from the fifty-fourth are wanting in the Vatican Septuagint, and sides with Kennicott and Pilkington that these are a rabbinic interpolation: read v.11 straight into v.32, he argues, and "there is nothing wanting to make the sense complete." The debate is thus squarely joined — Ellicott defending the received Hebrew as genuine repetition from parallel documents, Clarke doubting it as a later insertion. Yet neither lets the criticism dim the portrait: whether by one source or two, the shepherd-boy is drawn as the drudge of his family, kept low, and providentially sent toward the field where the honour no Israelite dares claim awaits him.
13:
The register of Jesse's fighting sons is brief, and only two witnesses linger over it, but they draw out its quiet purposes. The Reformed Gill reads the three eldest as men well disposed: they "went and followed Saul to the battle, either of their own accord, or rather at their father's motion," and were "forward, and some of the foremost that followed Saul," zealous to defend their king and country. The verse names them — Eliab the firstborn, Abinadab next, and Shammah the third — and Gill takes their readiness as creditable, whatever the envy that will presently show itself in Eliab.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the grammar and the names. The apparently redundant "had gone" after "went," they explain (following Bttcher), is needed to carry the force of the pluperfect: "the three eldest sons followed, had followed, Saul" — "a very ponderous construction, but quite correct, and even necessary, with the great deficiency of forms, to express the pluperfect." The point is chronological: the brothers were already in the field before David was sent. Keil then notes that the three names agree with the earlier list at the anointing (compare 1 Samuel 16:6-9), while the third, Shammah, appears under variant spellings elsewhere — Shimeah at 2 Samuel 13:3, and another form at 1 Chronicles 2:13 — a small witness to the way Hebrew proper names shift across the books.

There is no debate here; the two voices simply reinforce one another. Gill supplies the human note of the elder sons' zeal, Keil the philological care that fixes the verse as a pluperfect background clause. Together they let the register do its narrative work: it sets the three eldest in Saul's army, arms Eliab's coming resentment with the pride of the veteran over the errand-boy, and quietly prepares the reversal by which the youngest and least — the one left with the sheep — will accomplish what his soldier-brothers, standing in the ranks, dared not attempt. The list that honours the elder three is, in the witnesses' reading, the very frame against which David's greater honour will presently be measured.
14:
The narrator marks David out as the youngest, and the witnesses take the notice as the hinge on which the whole story turns. The Reformed Gill says plainly that the register of Jesse and his sons is given "for the sake of" this one: David "was the youngest, and after this makes a considerable figure in the camp and court of Saul." The verse closes the parenthesis about the three elder brothers precisely in order to bring the least of the eight to the front, and Gill hears in the arrangement the familiar biblical pattern by which the last is chosen for the greatest work.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the practical sense of David's movements. "David was going and returning away from Saul" — that is, he went backwards and forwards between the court and Bethlehem, feeding his father's sheep, "so that he was not in the permanent service of Saul, but at that very time was with his father." This careful reading resolves the seeming tension with the previous chapter: David's place as musician and armour-bearer did not bind him to a continuous residence, and he could be found among the flocks when the Philistines mustered. Keil then lets the notice run forward into the setting of the drama, observing that the Philistine "drew near morning and evening, and stationed himself for forty days," while Jesse, all unknowing, prepared to send his youngest into the camp.

The two witnesses do not differ; they read the verse as a deliberate narrowing of the lens. The elder three are dismissed to their ranks with a word, and the eye of the history settles on the shepherd. Gill draws the theological note that God's choice falls on the youngest and most overlooked; Keil supplies the chronological and geographical footing that makes the choice historically coherent, showing how the future king came to be at his father's sheepfold at the very hour the nation needed him. The smallness of David — youngest of eight, tender of the flock, going and returning on humble errands — is exactly the smallness the witnesses see God delighting to use, so that the coming victory over the giant may carry no whisper of human likelihood and every mark of divine appointment.
15:
This short verse is the chapter's great harmonizing note, and the witnesses treat it as the compiler's own answer to a difficulty he foresaw. The grammatical-historical Ellicott is most explicit: the statement that David "returned from Saul to feed his father's sheep" was introduced by the compiler "to show that, in spite of this apparent introduction of David into the history for the first time in this chapter, and the inquiry of King Saul from Abner respecting the young hero's father, he, the compiler, was perfectly aware that David had already visited the court of Saul in the capacity of a musician." Ellicott's larger thesis is that these historical books were made up from contemporaneous documents stored in the prophetic schools, so that certain favourite memories of David — the healing music and the first great exploit — were each preserved complete and occasionally overlap; "we have here many of the very words of the original narrative preserved to us."

The Reformed Gill works at the phrase "returned from Saul," which he renders "from above Saul," recalling Josephus's report that the physicians advised setting a man "over his head" to sing psalms and hymns to him. Being recovered by David's music, Saul had dismissed him, or given him leave to go home; so David was "going and coming," present at court when Saul had need of him and free to return to Bethlehem when he had not. The Wesleyan Wesley gives the same in a line: David went "from Saul's court, where having relieved Saul, he was permitted to go to his father's house, to be sent for again upon occasion."

The voices do not divide; they cooperate to defuse the apparent contradiction with the earlier account of David as court musician and armour-bearer (compare 1 Samuel 16:21-23). Their shared solution is that David's service was intermittent, not continuous — he came at need and went home at leisure — and that the historian, far from betraying ignorance of the previous chapter, has deliberately inserted this verse to keep the two stories in step. What some would read as a seam in the narrative the commentators read as the compiler's honesty, weaving two treasured memories of the shepherd-king without erasing the marks of either.
16:
The verse fixes the wearisome rhythm of the standoff — Goliath presenting himself morning and evening for forty days — and the witnesses read it partly as history and partly, in one striking case, as figure. The Reformed Gill catches a Jewish tradition in the timing: the giant "took those seasons on purpose to disturb them," coming at the hours when Israel recited the Shema, "Hear, O Israel," and said their morning and evening prayers. On this reading the daily defiance was aimed not merely at the army's courage but at its worship, an insult timed to the very confession of the God whom Goliath despised.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch keep to the narrative function of the notice. The remark "continues the description of Goliath's appearance and introduces the account which follows": while the Philistine came out every day for forty days with his challenge, Jesse sent David into the camp with an ephah of parched grains (see Leviticus 23:13) and ten loaves. The forty days are, for Keil, the measure of the deadlock that David's errand is about to break.

Here Ellicott preserves the chapter's one openly typological reading. Following Wordsworth, who follows Augustine, he sees in "forty days" a reference to the temptation of the true David, "who was in the wilderness forty days, tempted of the devil." The comment is quoted at length: "In David is Christ. Do not, therefore, read this history of David as if it did not concern you who are members of Christ." It is a specimen of the patristic school of exposition, reading the number as a shadow of the greater conflict in the wilderness.

The witnesses do not contend; they simply hear the verse on different registers. Gill and the Jewish tradition make the forty days a sustained assault on Israel's faith and prayer; Keil makes them the historical span within which the deliverer is quietly dispatched from Bethlehem; Ellicott and the fathers make them a type of the temptation of Christ. What unites the readings is a sense that the long, insolent regularity of the challenge was itself under providence — the giant "ripening for destruction," in the phrase the chapter keeps returning to, while God prepared, at the far end of the forty days, the unlikely champion who would silence him.
17:
Jesse's homely commission to David draws from the witnesses both a note on providence and a careful reading of the errand itself. The Reformed Gill marvels at the divine hand in the choice of messenger. Jesse "had three more at home, elder than David, and yet he is directed by the providence of God to pick and send him on the following errand, there being work for him to do Jesse knew nothing of." The father means only to victual his sons; God means to bring his anointed to the field. Gill hears in the ordinary sending of a younger son with bread the quiet machinery of heaven, moving the future king toward the giant under cover of a domestic chore.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the provisions and the terms. Jesse bids David "take now for thy brethren this ephah of parched grains" — the roasted corn of the harvest (compare Leviticus 23:13) — "and these ten loaves, and bring them quickly into the camp"; the command is urgent, "run to the camp." Keil reads the errand as wholly practical: a father's care for three sons on campaign, sending food by the one son still at home, with instructions to inquire after their welfare and bring back a token of it.

The two voices do not disagree; they read the same small scene at two depths. On the surface it is the plainest of family duties — a load of bread and parched grain carried on a boy's back to the front. Beneath it the commentators discern the purpose the father cannot see: that this errand of bread will set the shepherd within earshot of the giant's blasphemy at the exact hour Israel needs a deliverer. The witnesses let the humbleness of the task stand as itself part of the wonder. David is not summoned as a soldier or sent as a champion; he is dispatched as an errand-boy with provisions, and it is through so lowly a commission that God brings "the man after his own heart" to the valley of decision. The greatness that follows will owe nothing to David's rank and everything to a providence that works through loaves and cheeses.
18:
The commission continues with a present for the captain and a charge to bring back word of the brothers, and the witnesses read the domestic details closely. The Reformed Gill, following Jarchi, suggests the "captain of their thousand" — the chiliarch under whom Jesse's sons served — may have been Jonathan, who had commanded a thousand at Gibeah; the ten cheeses were a gift to secure his favour and good usage of the men under his charge. The Wesleyan Clarke adds an antiquarian gloss on the cheeses themselves: in the East "they do not make what we call cheese," but press the milk only slightly and carry it, highly salted, in rush baskets, "little different from curds" — the margin's "cheeses of milk."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch clarify the charge to "take their pledge." The word, variously rendered by the early translators, is best taken as a token that the brothers "are alive and well" — David is to visit them, inquire after their welfare, and bring home some pledge of their safety. The errand is thus one of provision and reassurance together, a father's anxiety for sons at the seat of war.

Ellicott here carries forward the patristic line he had opened at v.16, quoting Wordsworth's typology: "David is sent by his father to his brethren from Bethlehem. So the Divine David, Jesus Christ, who was born at Bethlehem, was sent to His brethren by his Heavenly Father." Wordsworth completes the figure by noting how David was ill-received by his brethren though he came at his father's bidding in kindness, "so Christ, when sent by His Father from heaven on an embassy of love, was ill-received by His own brethren, the Jews" (see John 1:11).

The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse on two levels at once. Gill and Clarke and Keil keep to the concrete world of loaves, cheeses, and a soldier's pledge, honouring the plain sense of a family's care; Ellicott lets the fathers hear beneath it a foreshadowing of the greater Son sent from Bethlehem to brethren who would not receive him. The homely errand is allowed to remain wholly homely, and yet, to the devout eye, to carry the shape of a larger sending still to come.
19:
This verse locates the brothers for David, and the witnesses agree that it belongs not to the narrator's frame but to Jesse's own speech. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch state it flatly: "But Saul and they and the whole of the men of Israel are in the terebinth valley" — this "forms part of Jesse's words," the closing clause of his directions telling David where to find his sons. Ellicott agrees, correcting the impression the English gives: the words "read in the English Version as an explanatory parenthesis," but "are really part of Jesse's direction to his shepherd son, telling him where he would find his brethren" — "they are in the valley of the Terebinth, fighting with the Philistines."

The Wesleyan Wesley guards the word "fighting" from misreading. It means, he says, "in a posture and readiness to fight with them," as the next verses explain — the two armies drawn up in array rather than actually engaged. The Reformed Gill adds only the identification of "they" as "the sons of Jesse and brethren of David," keeping the thread of the father's charge clear. Clarke, for his part, simply refers the reader to his long note at the chapter's end, where the whole of this section is weighed against the Septuagint.

The voices do not divide; their shared contribution is grammatical and steadying. By recognizing the verse as Jesse's speech, they remove an apparent redundancy and preserve the natural flow of the commission: the father names the provisions, names the captain, names the place, and sends his son. Wesley's qualification of "fighting" keeps the picture accurate — the hosts are poised, not yet locked in battle — which is exactly the situation into which David will walk, arriving to find the armies shouting for the fight but no blow yet struck. The small clarifications the witnesses offer here matter more than their brevity suggests: they keep the reader inside the tent at Bethlehem, hearing a father's instructions, rather than jumping ahead to a battlefield, so that the transition from sheepfold to valley of Elah is felt as one continuous, providentially guided errand.
20:
David sets out, and the witnesses commend both his diligence and the providence that times his arrival. The Puritan Matthew Henry makes much of the small marks of a faithful servant: "his prudence and care made him be up early, and yet not to leave his sheep without a keeper, so faithful was he in a few things, and therefore the fitter to be made ruler over many things, and so well had he learnt to obey before he pretended to command." God's providence, Henry adds, "brought him to the camp very seasonably," at the very hour both armies were setting the battle in array.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the scene of his arrival. Ellicott renders the place David came to not "the trench" but "the wagon rampart" — "a circle of wagons formed a rude fortification about the camp of Israel" — where he left his baggage before hastening to the front, where his brethren and the men of Judah would be posted (compare Numbers 10:14). Keil and Delitzsch catch the moment precisely: David reached the waggon-rampart "when the army, which was going out into battle array, raised the war-cry," and Israel and the Philistines drew up battle against battle — the clause set as a vivid circumstantial background to David's entrance.

The Wesleyan Wesley draws the providential lesson the others imply: "Jesse little thought of sending his son to the camp just at that critical juncture; but the wise God orders the time and all the circumstances of affairs, so as to serve the designs of his own glory." The Reformed Gill adds only that David rose early "being very ready and eager to obey his father's orders and visit his brethren."

The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as the meeting-point of David's faithfulness and God's timing. On his side, the exact obedience of a good shepherd — early, careful, leaving the flock safely kept; on God's side, the exquisite arrangement that brings the errand-boy to the rampart at the very instant the trumpet sounds. Henry's two observations belong together: the young man is faithful in little, and the wise God, who honours such fidelity, has ordered the hour so that the one sent with bread arrives just as the armies shout for the battle he alone will win.
21:
The verse repeats, almost formulaically, that "Israel and the Philistines had put the battle in array, army against army," and the witnesses read the doubled notice as the narrator's way of holding the reader at a moment of maximum tension. The Reformed Gill gives the plain sense: "both sides prepared for it, and drew up in line of battle." The two hosts, poised on their opposing heights with the ravine between, are once more fully arrayed — and yet, as through all the forty days, neither will advance.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the clause as the setting into which David steps. It is precisely "for Israel and the Philistines had put the battle in array" that explains the shout and the stir David meets on his arrival; the armies are drawn up, and into that charged stillness the shepherd runs "to inquire as to the health of his brethren." Keil treats the verse as a hinge: the last of the scene-setting before the young man's entrance changes everything.

The witnesses do not divide, and the material is spare, but the repetition itself carries their shared point. Twice now the narrator has marshalled the two armies face to face, and twice nothing has happened — the very stalemate that makes the daily challenge so galling and the deliverance so overdue. The Puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the whole arrival, notes that on this day "both sides were now preparing to fight," more likely to engage than at any point in the long deadlock, so that David's coming at just this juncture bears every mark of design. The commentators let the formula do its quiet work: it fixes the moment, freezes the massed battalions in their impotence, and sets the stage for the one figure who will break the array not by joining the ranks but by walking out alone into the valley between them. What the assembled thousands cannot do, arrayed in all their order, a single shepherd is about to do — and the twice-told array only sharpens the wonder of it. Keil's reading and Henry's converge here: the verse is at once the last frame of the deadlock and the first frame of the deliverance, the poised armies serving as the still background against which one unlikely figure will move. The commentators are content to let the formula stand unadorned, knowing that its very repetition presses home the paralysis of Israel's strength and prepares the reader to marvel that the reproach of forty days will be lifted, when it is lifted, not by the massed ranks in their array but by a boy with a sling.
22:
David reaches the camp, entrusts his load to the baggage-keeper, and runs to greet his brothers, and the witnesses find in the small verse both an antiquarian note and a portrait of a diligent servant. The Reformed Gill explains the practical action: David "left the load of provisions he brought with him in the hand of the keeper of the bag and baggage of the army," having no chance to deliver them to his brethren, "who were just going to engage in battle." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it the same way — David "left the vessels with the provisions in the charge of the keeper of the vessels, and ran into the ranks" to ask after his brothers' welfare.

Ellicott pauses on the word the English Version renders "carriage." It means, he explains, "his baggage," the term being used in this archaic sense elsewhere in Scripture — as at Isaiah 10:28, "he hath laid up his carriages," and at Acts 21:15, "we took up our carriages." The note guards the modern reader from imagining a vehicle where the text means only the bundle of loaves and cheeses David has carried on his back.

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the verse for character. David "honestly took care of" what he was charged with, leaving it with those responsible for the baggage; "but, though he had come a long journey with a great load, he ran into the army, to see what was doing there, and to pay his respects to his brethren." Henry hears in this brisk and lively obedience the mark of a man fit for advancement: "Seest thou a man thus diligent in his business? he is in the way of preferment; he shall stand before kings" (compare Proverbs 22:29).

The witnesses do not differ; they read a single unhurried scene at complementary angles. Gill and Keil secure the plain action and the care of the trust; Ellicott clears away a verbal difficulty; Henry draws the moral, that the same faithfulness which kept the sheep and guarded the baggage is the seed of the greatness to come. The young man who runs eagerly into the ranks, load safely stowed, is the one Providence is bringing to the front — diligent in a small thing, and so being made ready to stand before the king.
23:
As David talks with his brothers, Goliath comes up and repeats his challenge, and David hears it — and the witnesses give most of their attention to a textual crux and a famous problem of identity. The Reformed Gill keeps to the scene: David was speaking with his brethren "about their health, and the errand he came upon," when the champion drew near. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note a probable copyist's slip in the Hebrew — a word for "ranks" written imperfectly, which the Keri, the Septuagint, and the Vulgate correct — and confirm that "David heard it," the challenge falling for the first time on the ears of the one man in Israel it will rouse.

Ellicott raises the difficulty over the giant's very name. In 2 Samuel 21:19 we read that "Goliath of Gath, the staff of whose spear was like a weaver's beam," was slain by Elhanan the Bethlehemite, after David had become king. Ellicott offers two solutions: either "Goliath was a general designation of these monstrous descendants of the ancient Anakim in Gath," or, with Ewald, "the name Goliath really only belongs to the giant slain by Elhanan," and was transferred in error to the champion whom David slew — "who is, moreover, generally called simply 'the Philistine.'" It is a genuine puzzle, and Ellicott records the competing answers without dogmatism.

The witnesses do not sharply divide, but they distribute the labour. Gill preserves the human moment — a young man's conversation broken off by the giant's approach; Keil steadies the Hebrew text; Ellicott confronts the harmonizing question raised by the later book. Behind the discussion lies a shared confidence that the narrative is coherent and the champion real, whatever the tangle over his name in the parallel history. What matters for the story, they agree, is the collision the verse arranges: the boastful defiance that has silenced a nation for forty days now sounds in the hearing of the shepherd from Bethlehem. The giant repeats "according to the same words," unchanged and unanswered; but this time a new listener is standing in the ranks, and the long deadlock is about to be broken by the one man whom the blasphemy will drive not to flight but to holy anger.
24:
The men of Israel flee at Goliath's approach, "sore afraid," and the witnesses probe the strangeness of a whole army's cowardice. The Reformed Gill observes that they fled "even as it should seem before they heard him," knowing already, from forty days' experience, what he would say. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the collective force of the Hebrew and let the men's own words carry the fear forward into the report of Saul's reward.

Ellicott confronts the puzzle head-on: "the student of the history can hardly understand this great fear of a giant Philistine which seems to have come upon the warriors of Saul." Remembering the gallant deeds of the people in former years, "it reads like a page out of the story of another race." His explanation is moral and providential: "a dull, cowardly torpor had come over Saul, the punishment for his self-will and disobedience, and the king's helpless lethargy had settled now on the hearts of the soldiers he had trained so well in his earlier and nobler days." The panic of the army is the shadow of the king's forsakenness.

The Wesleyan Wesley states the theological arithmetic that the others imply: "one Philistine could never have thus put ten thousand Israelites to flight, unless their Rock, being forsaken by them, had justly sold them and shut them up" — the language drawn straight from Moses' song (compare Deuteronomy 32:30). The flight of the many before the one is not a fact of ordinary warfare but a sign of covenant abandonment.

The witnesses do not divide; they read the rout as a spiritual, not a merely military, event. An army that had once been bold is now unmanned, and the commentators trace the cause with one voice to the withdrawal of God from Saul and from the nation that followed him. The verse thus deepens the sense of hopelessness the chapter has been building — no man will stand, the ranks scatter at the mere sight of the giant — precisely so that the coming victory may be unmistakably God's own. Where a whole host has been "sold" and "shut up" for its unfaithfulness, only the Rock they forsook can deliver, and he will do it through a single shepherd who has not forgotten to reckon with him.
25:
The Israelites rehearse Saul's lavish offer to whoever will kill the giant — great riches, the king's daughter, and freedom for the victor's house — and the witnesses examine both the credibility and the meaning of the promise. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch face the fact that "there is nothing said afterwards about the fulfilment of these promises." Yet they refuse to dismiss the report as popular exaggeration: since the people repeat it again in v.27, "in all probability Saul had actually made some such promises, but did not feel himself bound to fulfil them afterwards, because he had not made them expressly" to David alone.

The chief interest gathers on the phrase "make his father's house free in Israel," and here Ellicott canvasses the range. The simplest meaning is freedom from personal service in the army and elsewhere — "what in mediaeval history is known by the general term Corvée" — together with exemption from taxation or forced war-contributions. The Wesleyan Wesley gives just this sense: "free from all those tributes and charges which either the court or the camp required." Ellicott then reports Ewald's bolder view, that the promise included the elevation of the victorious house to noble rank, henceforth "freeholders," an intermediate stage between king and simple subject — though Ellicott doubts that Saul's government was "as yet sufficiently organised" to justify so developed a reading. The Reformed Gill keeps to the plain scene of soldiers repeating the royal bounty to one another.

The debate over "free" is real but bounded, turning on how much social advancement the word implies and whether the promise was ever meant to bind. What the witnesses share is a certain irony in the offer. Saul, who dares not fight the giant himself, bids high for a substitute, dangling riches, marriage into the royal house, and lasting privilege — and the narrative quietly records that these promises "were never fulfilled by Saul in the case of the house of Jesse." The commentators let the discrepancy stand as a first quiet mark against the king: he is generous with rewards he will not honour, staking his daughter and his treasury on a courage he lacks, while the man who will actually take away Israel's reproach is moved, as the next verse shows, by something the reward-list never mentions — the honour of the living God.
26:
David breaks his silence, asking what the killer of the Philistine shall receive and, in the same breath, "who is this uncircumcised Philistine, that he should defy the armies of the living God?" — and the witnesses hear in the double question the first note of the chapter's true theme. The Puritan Matthew Henry reads David's zeal with care. Two things "fired him with a holy indignation": that the challenger "was one that was uncircumcised, a stranger to God and out of covenant with him," and that the challenged were "the armies of the living God, devoted to him, employed by him and for him, so that the affronts offered to them reflected upon the living God himself, and that he could not bear." Henry notes too David's modesty: he did not presume on his court acquaintance to offer his service directly, remembering the proverb he would one day write, "Put not forth thyself in the presence of the king" (Proverbs 25:6), but let his zeal be reported until it reached Saul's ear.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch catch the same fire in the Hebrew. David's words "gave rise to the supposition that he wanted to go and fight with this Philistine himself," for he speaks of the giant as "this uncircumcised one," standing outside the covenant with Jehovah, who has dared to insult "the ranks of the living God." Keil quotes the Berleburger Bible: the idolater "must know that he has not to do with men, but with a living God." Ellicott paints the scene vividly — the dismayed soldiers leaning gloomily on their spears, and "the enthusiastic shepherd boy, glowing with religious fervour, going from group to group of the advanced guard, asking questions, and gleaning all the information possible about this insulter of his God." The Reformed Gill keeps the plain frame: David spoke "to the men that stood by him," next to him and within hearing of the giant.

The witnesses do not divide; they converge on the phrase that transforms the whole standoff. Where the army has heard only a fearful champion, David hears an "uncircumcised Philistine" affronting "the living God." That single confession reframes the contest: it is no longer a duel Israel dare not risk but a blasphemy that cannot be borne. The commentators agree that David's question about the reward is subordinate to his question about the defiance — he asks what is offered not from ambition but to have his indignation noticed and carried to the king. The reproach that has cowed a nation becomes, in the mouth of one shepherd, an intolerable insult to the God of the covenant, and that is the spark from which the deliverance will catch.
27:
The people repeat the promised reward to David, and the witnesses treat the brief exchange as the confirmation of what he has already heard and the deepening of his resolve. The Reformed Gill gives the plain sense: they "told him what was proposed to be done in honour to the man that should attempt to kill him, and succeed." The soldiers answer David "after this manner, saying, So shall it be done to the man that killeth him" — the same threefold bounty of riches, royal marriage, and a freed house, now voiced a second time in David's hearing.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attach weight to this very repetition. It is because "the people assured him again of the same thing" that Keil declines to write off the reward as a mere popular exaggeration; the promise was real, twice reported, whatever Saul's later failure to honour it. The verse thus does quiet evidentiary work: the offer stands attested by the men themselves, and David's inquiry is answered plainly enough to leave no doubt of the terms.

The witnesses do not divide, and the material is spare, but they let the doubling carry its dramatic charge. The Puritan Matthew Henry, following David through these verses, observes how he asked "some" and then "others" what the reward would be, "not because he was ambitious of the honour, but because he would have it taken notice of, and reported to Saul, how much he resented the indignity hereby done to Israel and Israel's God." On this reading the second telling of the reward is less about the prize than about the growing report: David is deliberately letting his holy indignation circulate through the ranks until it must come to the king. The commentators see in the people's ready answer a small providence — the very soldiers who flee the giant are, without knowing it, forwarding the cause of the champion who will fell him, passing along the terms that will bring David before Saul. The reward that no man in the army will earn is being pressed upon the one man who cares nothing for it and everything for the honour of the living God.
28:
Eliab overhears his youngest brother and rounds on him with bitter words, and the witnesses read the outburst as a study in envy and a trial of David's meekness. The Puritan Matthew Henry is fullest. Eliab's abuse he traces to jealousy: the elder brother, vexed "that his younger brother should speak those bold words against the Philistine which he himself durst not say," feared that David's honour would eclipse his own, "and therefore, such is the nature of jealousy, he would rather that Goliath should triumph over Israel than that David should be the man that should triumph over him." Henry finds the charge doubly unjust — David had left the sheep "in good hands," yet is tauntingly asked "with whom hast thou left those few sheep?" — and cruelly ungrateful, since David came in kindness at their father's bidding. "Wrath is cruel and anger is outrageous, but who can stand before envy" (compare Proverbs 27:4), "especially the envy of a brother" (see Proverbs 18:19).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the sneer sharply: "with whom hast thou left those few sheep" — as if the loss of one would be a great loss — "I know thy presumption and the wickedness of thy heart; for thou hast come down to look at the war." Eliab, Keil observes, "sought for the splinter in his brother's eye": the man aspires beyond his lowly calling and delights in bloodshed. Ellicott supplies the back-story: many years lay between eldest and youngest, and "this jealous anger was, no doubt, no new thing in Eliab," who perhaps remembered how Samuel had passed him by and anointed this young brother (compare 1 Samuel 16:13) — was David now come "with power unknown to him to perform some startling deed of daring?" The Wesleyan Wesley glosses "naughtiness" as "thy false-confidence and vain-glorious curiosity," and exclaims at "the folly and wickedness of envy! How groundless its jealousies are, how unjust its censures, how unfair its representations! God preserve us from such a spirit."

The witnesses do not contend; they read one scene with one mind. Eliab's fury is groundless, ungrateful, and self-betraying — the accusation of pride is itself the projection of a proud and envious heart. And they agree that the moment is providentially a test: the future king, falsely charged by his own brother on the eve of his greatest service, is being proved in patience before he is proved in battle, so that his conquest of Goliath may be crowned by the harder conquest of his own resentment.
29:
David answers Eliab's abuse with a single mild question, and the witnesses divide gently over how to render it. The Wesleyan Clarke prefers the deprecating sense: the Hebrew, he believes, means "I have spoken but a word," and he adds the pointed question, "should a man be made an offender for a word?" Ellicott gives both readings fairly. The Hebrew "would be more literally rendered, 'Is it not a word,' or 'It was only a word,'" David thus softening his brother's anger — "What have I done? It was but a mere word. I was only speaking with holy anger about this impious challenge of the Philistine; nothing more." But if we keep the Authorised Version's "Is there not a cause?" then "the sense is quite clear": surely there is good cause for passionate emotion at such an insult to Israel's God. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read it the first way — "What have I done, then? It was only a word" — a modest reply "so as to put the scorn of his reprover to shame."

The Wesleyan Wesley, by contrast, presses the bolder sense of "a cause": "Of my thus speaking? Is this giant invincible? Is our God unable to oppose him and subdue him?" On Wesley's reading the question is not merely a plea of innocence but a spark of faith, and he draws the lesson that "David is not deterred from his undertaking by the hard words of Eliab. They that undertake public services must not think it strange if they be opposed by those from whom they had reason to expect assistance, but must humbly go on with their work, in the face not only of their enemies' threats, but of their friends' slights, suspicions, and censures."

The difference among the witnesses is one of shading, not substance: whether David chiefly deprecates his brother's wrath ("it was only a word") or chiefly justifies his zeal ("is there not a cause?"). Either way they agree on the temper the answer displays. David "did not render railing for railing, but with a soft answer turned away his brother's wrath" — a conquest, as Matthew Henry says elsewhere, "in some respects more honourable than his conquest of Goliath." The commentators let the mildness stand as the mark of the man: unshaken in purpose, unprovoked in speech, he turns from the quarrel and presses on toward the giant, refusing to be diverted from the honour of God by the injustice of a brother.
30:
Rebuffed by Eliab, David simply turns to another and asks the same question, receiving the same answer, and the witnesses read his quiet persistence as the working-out of a providential design. The Reformed Gill marks the small movement: David "turned from his brother Eliab to another, right against him, and directed his discourse to him," undeterred by the rebuke. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the thread forward: David "spake after the same manner, and the people answered him again after the former manner," until his words "were told to Saul, who had him sent for immediately." The repetition is not aimless; it is the channel by which David's zeal reaches the king.

The Wesleyan Wesley draws out the inward and the providential together. David "tarried," Wesley says, "being secretly moved by God's Spirit to undertake the combat"; and "he speaks with divers persons about it, that it might come to the king's ear." On this reading the shepherd's persistence is neither idle curiosity nor mere boldness but the outworking of a divine impulse: he keeps raising the matter, group after group, precisely so that the report of one man willing to fight will make its way up to Saul.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse is brief, but they let it register two things at once. First, David's unshaken constancy: turned away by his own brother's scorn, he neither argues nor sulks but calmly repeats his inquiry to the next man, refusing to let the honour of God be dropped because a relative has taken offence. Second, the hidden hand behind his conduct: what looks like a young man's stubborn questioning is, in the commentators' eyes, the Spirit of God moving him to keep the cause alive until it reaches the one who can commission him. The people's unchanging answer and David's unchanging question together do the narrative's work, carrying his resolve from the ranks to the royal tent. By the end of the verse the report has done its errand; Saul has heard, and sends for the shepherd who alone in all the camp will stand for the armies of the living God.
31:
David's words, passed from mouth to mouth, at last reach the king, and the witnesses read the moment as the point where a shepherd's zeal becomes a royal commission. The Reformed Gill traces the path plainly: the things David spoke "to one, and to another" were "reported to other persons, and so they went from one to another through many hands" until they came to Saul. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch complete the sentence: David's words "were told to Saul, who had him sent for immediately." The long chain of report, begun when David first questioned the men in the ranks, closes here; the king who dares not fight sends for the one who will.

Ellicott fills in what the terse verse omits. "No doubt much more was said by the brave shepherd boy than the compiler of the history has preserved for us in the brief account here." Ellicott believes David "felt that supernatural strength had been communicated to him by the Spirit of God, which came upon him on the day of his anointing" (compare 1 Samuel 16:13), "and it is probable that he had openly avowed his earnest desire of meeting the dreaded foe face to face" — a desire so remarkable that it was reported to Saul.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, following David to the royal presence, marks the reversal the verse quietly accomplishes: "a little shepherd, come but this morning from keeping sheep, has more courage than all the mighty men of Israel." He draws from it the recurring lesson that "God often sends good words to his Israel, and does great things for them, by the weak and foolish things of the world." The verse is the turning of the tide: the report that no soldier would act upon has produced one volunteer, and he is summoned before the king.

The witnesses do not divide; they read the summons as the fruit of David's Spirit-given boldness working through the ordinary means of rumour and report. What began as a young man's holy indignation, spoken to whoever stood near, has become a matter for the throne. Ellicott's sense that the record is compressed and Henry's sense of the marvellous reversal belong together: heaven has moved a shepherd to speak, and the speaking has carried him, by the plainest human channels, into the presence of the king he is about to deliver.
32:
Brought before Saul, David speaks his first recorded words in the royal presence — "Let no man's heart fail because of him; thy servant will go and fight with this Philistine" — and the witnesses admire both their courage and their courtesy. The Reformed Gill notes that David had perceived how the hearts of all, even the king's, had failed, since none dared meet the giant, and (following Kimchi) that in part he "said this within himself" before uttering it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the sense exactly: "Let no man's courage fail on his account; thy servant will go and fight with this Philistine." The Wesleyan Clarke observes that the verse "properly connects with the eleventh," reading straight on from Israel's dismay to David's offer — the point on which he rests his larger theory that the intervening verses are a later insertion.

The Puritan Matthew Henry and the Wesleyan Wesley together catch the delicacy of David's phrasing. "It would have reflected too much upon the valour of his prince if he had said, Let not thy heart fail," Henry observes; "therefore he speaks generally, Let no man's heart fail." Wesley says the same: "It would have reflected upon his prince to say, Let not thy heart fail; therefore he speaks in general terms." Both then sound the note of wonder that runs through the chapter: "a little shepherd, come but this morning from keeping sheep, has more courage than all the mighty men of Israel," and "thus doth God often do great things for his people by the weak things of the world."

The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as a small masterpiece of faith and tact. David undertakes what a whole army has declined, yet frames his offer so as to spare the king's honour, veiling Saul's cowardice under a general exhortation. The commentators hear in "thy servant will go" the quiet confidence of a man who reckons with God rather than with the giant's bulk, and in "let no man's heart fail" the courtesy of one who will rebuke no one, least of all his king, even while shaming the fear of all. Henry and Wesley let the contrast stand at its full height: the courage that has drained from ten thousand trained soldiers is found entire in the youngest and least, freshly come from the sheepfold — proof that boldness is God's gift, lodged where he pleases, and that he delights to work his greatest deliverances through the most unlikely hands.
33:
Saul's reply — "thou art not able to go against this Philistine, for thou art but a youth, and he a man of war from his youth" — draws from the witnesses a note on David's age and a reading of the king's divided mind. The Reformed Gill gives Saul's plain objection: David "had neither strength of body nor skill in military affairs to encounter with a man of his stature and warlike genius and practice." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch summarize the exchange: to Saul's objection that a mere youth could not fight a lifelong warrior, David answered with the story of the lion and the bear.

On David's age the commentators differ mildly. Clarke supposes him "about twenty-two or twenty-three years of age," while Wesley makes him "not above twenty years old, a novice, a raw and unexperienced soldier." The difference is small, and both agree on the substance: by ordinary reckoning David was far too young and untried to face Goliath, and Saul's doubt was, on natural grounds, entirely reasonable.

Ellicott reads the king's heart as divided between admiration and misgiving. Saul "evidently looked on the brave boy with love and admiration, but at first doubted in his heart the reality of David's mission." More than anyone in the camp, Ellicott thinks, Saul "felt that David possessed powers not usually bestowed on the sons of men, and was clearly disposed from the first to grant the shepherd boy's startling petition that the honour of Israel might be entrusted to his almost child-hands." Yet he would "talk with him, and set before him the grave perils of the terrible encounter he was so eager to engage in."

The witnesses do not sharply divide; the disagreement over David's exact years is incidental to their shared reading of the scene. Saul's objection voices the whole logic of the flesh that has paralysed the army: strength must be met with strength, experience with experience, and by that measure David is disqualified before he begins. The commentators let the king state the natural impossibility in its full force — a boy against a veteran giant — precisely so that David's coming answer may shift the ground entirely. Where Saul reckons youth against years and sees defeat, David will reckon the living God against an uncircumcised Philistine and see certain victory. The verse sets the two calculations side by side, and prepares the reader to watch faith overturn the arithmetic of war.
34:
David answers Saul's objection with the story of the lion and the bear, and the witnesses read it as at once a plain record of past courage and a parable of the faith that now moves him. The Reformed Gill notes that the answer "is founded on experience and facts, and shows that he was not so weak and inexpert as Saul took him to be." The Wesleyan Clarke lays out David's reasoning as a numbered argument: he had courage and strength, both already tried; a lion had seized a lamb and he had slain it, and a bear likewise; and this Philistine, "an uncircumcised man, an enemy to God," would be no different, for "God will not be on his side."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the Hebrew: the definite article before "lion" and "bear" points to "the well-known beasts of prey," and the grammar subordinates the bear to the lion, "not as implying that the lion and the bear went together in search of prey," but adding the second as a further instance of the same deliverance. Ellicott hears in the whole speech "that simple brave narrative which children listen to with glowing cheeks, bearing the stamp of truth on every word": fierce wild animals had attacked his flock, and he had met and slain them "almost without arms," helped by an invisible Guardian who, he felt sure, "would give him nerve and strength now in this more dangerous encounter."

The Puritan Matthew Henry lifts the shepherd's tale toward its deeper meaning. David "tells his story like a man of spirit," not ashamed of the calling his brother had just despised, but fetching from it the very experience that now animates him. In his care for the flock — venturing his life to rescue a single lamb — Henry sees a man "fit to be a king, to whom the lives of subjects should be dear," and "fit to be a type of Christ, the good Shepherd," who gathers the lambs in his arms (compare Isaiah 40:11) and lays down his life for the sheep.

The witnesses do not divide; they read the memory as the ground of David's confidence. Gill and Clarke stress the factual basis — tried courage, proven strength; Keil secures the text; Ellicott draws out the note of the unseen Helper; Henry finds in the good shepherd a shadow of the greater Shepherd to come. All agree that David appeals not to prowess as such but to a pattern of deliverance already experienced, which he is about to press, in the next verse, into an argument of pure faith.
35:
David presses the story to its vivid climax — he went out after the beast, smote it, delivered the lamb from its mouth, and when it turned on him caught it by the beard and killed it — and the witnesses, though few here, draw out both the action and the difficulty in it. The Reformed Gill infers from the mention of the beard that "a lion rather is meant," since the seizing of the mane by the shaggy locks fits the lion best. The scene is one of astonishing daring: an unarmed shepherd, pursuing a predator that has robbed his flock, snatching the prey from its jaws and then grappling the animal to death.

The Wesleyan Clarke takes up a small grammatical question the verse raises. He judges that "the slaying of the lion and the bear mentioned here must have taken place at two different times," and would read the sentence accordingly: "I went out after him — the lion — and smote him; and when he — the bear — rose up against me, I caught him by the beard and slew him." On Clarke's reading the single sentence compresses two distinct encounters, each a separate proof of the courage and strength David has claimed before Saul.

The witnesses do not divide, and the material is spare, but the verse rewards attention beyond its length. What David describes is not a lucky escape but a settled habit of valour: he did not merely frighten the beast off, but pursued it, recovered the lamb alive, and when the creature reared against him met it hand to hand and destroyed it. Gill's note on the beard and Clarke's on the two occasions together fill out the picture of a shepherd who had, more than once, staked his own life against a lion or a bear for the sake of a single sheep. The commentators let the concrete details carry their weight: the mouth that held the lamb, the beard gripped in a boy's hand, the beast slain outright. These are the credentials David lays before the king — not the trophies of the battlefield but the unwitnessed courage of the sheepfold, where, as the next verse will make explicit, the same God who saved him from the lion's paw was preparing him to face the giant. The deed done in obscurity becomes the ground of the deed that will be done before all Israel.
36:
