David draws the moral of his shepherd-stories: he has slain both lion and bear, "and this uncircumcised Philistine shall be as one of them, seeing he hath defied the armies of the living God." The witnesses read the comparison as the heart of David's confidence. The Reformed Gill takes "both the lion and the bear" to mean many such beasts, killed "at different times, whenever any of them came into the flock," so that the phrase denotes a settled experience of victory rather than a single feat; on this ground David reckons the giant no more formidable than the predators he has repeatedly overcome.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the natural-historical note that "the lion and the bear were, in the days of Saul, common in Palestine," the country then being densely wooded, and bears still numerous in the wilder districts. But he fastens on the theological weight of the comparison, quoting the Berleburger Bible on "shall be as one of them": the idolater "must know that he has not to do with mere men, but with God — with a living God will he have to do, and not with a lifeless idol." That single phrase, "the living God," is for the witnesses the pivot of the whole speech. The giant has ranged himself not merely against Israel's soldiers but against Israel's God, and by that act he has forfeited the fear he inspires and become, in David's eyes, one more beast to be struck down.

The witnesses do not divide; they converge on the logic of faith that governs the verse. David does not compare himself to Goliath — youth to veteran, boy to giant — but compares Goliath to a lion and a bear, and himself, in both cases, to the instrument of a God who delivers. The uncircumcised state of the Philistine marks him as outside the covenant, a stranger to the living God whom he has dared to defy; and the defiance, far from making him invincible, is precisely what seals his fate. The commentators let the comparison stand as the reversal of Saul's arithmetic: where the king weighed strength against strength, David weighs a covenant-breaker against the covenant Lord, and finds the outcome as certain as the death of the beasts that once threatened his flock. The one who defies the living God has already, in David's reckoning, made himself as good as slain.
37:
David completes his argument — "the Lord that delivered me out of the paw of the lion and the bear, he will deliver me out of the hand of this Philistine" — and Saul, convinced, sends him with a blessing. The witnesses read the verse as the summit of David's faith and the momentary return of the better Saul. The Reformed Gill stresses that David ascribes his past victories "not to any natural strength or skill of his, but to the power of God, of whose assistance he made no question." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch locate the ground of his courage exactly: it "rested upon his confident belief that the living God would not let His people be defied" with impunity.

The Wesleyan Wesley sharpens the difference between the past deliverances and the present cause: "the lion and the bear were only enemies to me and my sheep, and it was in defence of them I attacked them; but this Philistine is an enemy to my God and his people, and it is for their honour that I attack him." The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the abiding lesson: "our experiences ought to be improved by us as our encouragements to trust in God and venture in the way of duty. He that has delivered does and will." He recalls how Paul reasoned in the same way — "I was delivered out of the mouth of the lion, and therefore, I trust, the Lord shall deliver me" (compare 2 Timothy 4:17).

On Saul's blessing the witnesses hear an echo of his former self. Clarke says simply that "Saul saw that these were reasonable grounds of confidence, and therefore wished him success." Ellicott hears more: "Go, and the Lord be with thee" recalls "the Saul of old days, before the covenant between him and the Mighty One of Israel was broken," a great act of courageous trust — for this was no mere duel but a combat on which "the fortunes of the nation for an indefinite period were to be staked."

The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as faith reasoning from experience to assurance. The God who saved in the sheepfold will save in the valley, for the cause is now his own and the enemy has defied him. And in Saul's parting word the commentators catch a poignant flicker of the king he once was, blessing in the name of the Lord the very champion who will one day inherit his crown.
38:
Saul arms David in his own war-gear, and the witnesses read the gesture as well-meant but mistaken — the flesh trying to equip a victory that will belong to faith. The Reformed Gill is careful to say this was "not what Saul wore himself," for a king would not strip off his armour on the eve of battle, and in any case "what suited the one would not be fit for the other, their bulk and stature being different"; it was rather spare armour Saul had brought "to furnish any that might want it." The Wesleyan Wesley agrees, taking it for "some military vestments then used in war and contrived for defence, such as buff-coats are now," drawn from the king's armoury.

The Wesleyan Clarke reads Saul's motive charitably: the king "knew that although the battle was the Lord's, yet prudent means should be used to secure success." Ellicott develops the point at length. Saul "was determined to omit no earthly means of securing victory to his young champion," and so made him try on "the best-tempered and costliest" array the camp possessed. Ellicott notes that the word rendered "his armour" "literally signifies his garments," the dress worn beneath the mail, upon which the heavy metal suit, the great sword, and the royal helm were buckled. He adds that David need not have been of Saul's proportions, since much of the dress could be adjusted; yet the result showed the whole "far too heavy and cumbersome" for one slighter and shorter than the king.

The witnesses do not divide; they read the scene as a gentle irony. Saul, who will not fight, is anxious that his substitute be armed to the utmost, loading the shepherd with the very apparatus of war that the whole army's confidence has rested on and been shamed by. The commentators let the well-intentioned gesture stand as a foil: the king trusts in tempered brass and a mighty sword, and would have David trust in them too. But the deliverance God is preparing must carry no such credentials, lest the glory be shared with human armour. In quietly stressing that the gear was borrowed, ill-fitting, and burdensome, the witnesses prepare the reader for David's refusal in the next verse — the setting-aside of Saul's panoply so that it may plainly appear, when the giant falls, that the victory was the Lord's and not the armourer's.
39:
David girds on the sword, tries to walk, and puts the whole equipment off with the words, "I cannot go with these, for I have not proved them" — and the witnesses read the refusal as wisdom, not weakness. The Reformed Gill notes that Saul "perhaps furnished him" with the sword too, completing the borrowed array. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch picture the moment: "thus equipped with brazen helmet, coat of mail, and sword, David began to walk, but soon found that he could do nothing with these," and so laid them aside and took up instead his shepherd's staff, five smooth stones, and his sling.

The Wesleyan commentators explain why the untested gear had to go. Clarke observes that "in ancient times it required considerable exercise and training to make a man expert in the use of such heavy armour," which in his own day scarcely a man could even carry; unaccustomed to it, David found it "an encumbrance." Wesley gives the same in a phrase: "I have no skill or experience in the management of this kind of arms." The point is not that the armour was bad but that it was foreign to the man who must fight in it.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the enduring lesson. "Those that aim at things above their education and usage, and covet the attire and armour of princes, forget that that is the best for us which we are fit for and accustomed to." He presses the wider application: "it is not to be inquired how excellent any thing is, but how proper. Let Saul's coat be ever so rich, and his armour ever so strong, what is David the better if they fit him not?" And he lifts it higher still: this was "from the Lord, that it might more plainly appear he fought and conquered in faith, and that the victory was from Him who works by the feeblest and most despised means."

The witnesses do not divide; they read David's refusal as both practical sense and providential design. Practically, a warrior must fight with weapons he has proved, not with another man's untried panoply. Providentially, the stripping-off of the royal armour clears the field of every human credential, so that the deliverance may wear no armour but faith. Henry's contrast between the excellent and the proper is the verse's abiding word: David lays down what is grand but unfitting, and takes up what is humble but his own, that God alone may have the glory.
40:
David takes his staff, five smooth stones, and his sling, and goes to meet the giant, and the witnesses gather around both the plain wisdom of his choice and its deeper meanings. The Reformed Gill notes that David "chose rather to appear in the habit of a shepherd than of a soldier," carrying the very staff he used in keeping the flock. The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the contrast in arms: against the giant's helmet, coat of mail, sword, and spear, David has "no breastplate but his plain shepherd's coat, no spear but his staff, no sword nor bow but his sling, no quiver but his scrip," and for arrows "five smooth stones picked up out of the brook." By this, Henry says, "it appeared that his confidence was purely in the power of God, and not in any sufficiency of his own," and that God, who put it into his heart to fight, "put it into his head with what weapons to do it."

The Wesleyan Clarke supplies the practical genius of the choice, describing the sling as "a powerful offensive weapon" and citing the ancient Baleares, who from childhood learned to sling stones "at an hair's breadth"; David, "evidently an expert marksman," held an advantage "of which the giant does not seem to have been aware" — like a man "armed with a rifle, while his enemy had only a spear and a sword." The stones were smooth, Clarke adds, so as to fly true and leave the sling readily. Wesley gathers the reasons David chose such contemptible arms: because he had no skill in others, because he had inward assurance of victory even by these, and because "such a conquest would be more honourable to God, and most shameful and discouraging to the Philistines."

Here Ellicott preserves the chapter's fullest typology. Following Wordsworth and Augustine, he reads the five smooth stones out of the brook as a figure of the Gospel conflict: "So our Divine David, the Good Shepherd of Bethlehem, when He went forth at the temptation to meet Satan, our ghostly Goliath, chose five stones out of the brook. He took the five books of Moses out of the flowing stream of Judaism. He took what was solid out of what was fluid, what was permanent out of what was transitory"; and "with this sling and stone of Scripture He laid our Goliath low," prefacing each reply to the tempter with "It is written" (the words drawn from Deuteronomy).

The witnesses do not divide; they read one action on several levels. Gill, Henry, Clarke, and Wesley see a shepherd wisely fighting with the weapons he has proved, his very simplicity proclaiming that the victory is God's; Ellicott and the fathers hear beneath it the greater Shepherd overthrowing the greater enemy with the stones of Scripture. All agree that the sling and the smooth stones, so despised in appearance, are exactly the means God has chosen to shame the giant's brass and magnify his own arm.
41:
The two combatants close the distance, and the witnesses read the giant's advance as a study in ponderous pride. The Reformed Gill pictures him coming on "by slow paces, because of the weight of his armour and the bulk of his body, yet with a haughty air and a proud gait." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the mounting tension in a single line: "the Philistine came closer and closer to David," the shield-bearer going before him. The Wesleyan Wesley supposes that "a signal was made that his challenge was accepted," bringing the giant forward at last after forty days of unanswered defiance.

The detail of the shield-bearer draws the commentators' notice. The Puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the encounter, observes that Goliath came on "as if he distrusted his helmet and coat of mail," with "a man going before him carrying his shield," his own hands being full with his sword and spear. Clarke refers the reader back to his note on the great shield at v.7, the huge zinnah borne in front of the warrior; and he observes elsewhere that this verse too is absent from the Septuagint, part of the section he judged a later addition.

The witnesses do not divide; the verse is brief, but they let it frame the visual contrast on which the whole scene turns. On one side, a mountain of a man, weighed down with brass, advancing slowly behind a servant who carries his shield; on the other, as the following verses will show, an unarmoured boy hastening lightly to meet him. The commentators sense the irony already gathering: the giant's very equipment, the mass of metal that makes him terrible, also makes him slow, and the shield that goes before him leaves his face exposed. Henry's suggestion that Goliath "distrusted" his own armour is telling — the man clad in brass at every point still needs another to bear his shield. The stately, overloaded approach that is meant to overawe is quietly being set up, in the witnesses' reading, as the prelude to a fall, the more humiliating for all the pomp with which it begins. The champion who has kept an army trembling comes on in full panoply, unaware that his slowness and his uncovered brow are about to become his undoing.
42:
Goliath at last looks upon his challenger, and the witnesses read his contempt as the pride that goes before destruction. The Reformed Gill pictures the giant looking about for an antagonist "much like himself," and, seeing instead "a puny young man," despising him in his heart, perhaps taking it "as an affront to him to send such a man to fight with him." The Wesleyan Clarke says he "held him in contempt," seeing his youth and from "his ruddy complexion supposed him to be effeminate"; Wesley adds that David had "not so much as the countenance of a martial person."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the moment in the giant's own scornful words: "he looked at him contemptuously, because he was a youth, and then said to him, Am I a dog, that thou comest to me with sticks?" — the plural used "in contemptuous exaggeration of the armour of David, which appeared so thoroughly unfit for the occasion" — and "cursed David by his God," so defying not the boy only but the God of Israel, and finished with the threat to give his flesh to the birds and beasts.

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the disdain most fully. Goliath "looked about, expecting to meet some tall strong man, but when he saw what a mean figure he made with whom he was to engage, he disdained him, thought it below him to enter the lists with him, fearing that the contemptibleness of the champion he contended with would lessen the glory of his victory." He took note that David was "but a youth, not come to his strength, ruddy and of a fair countenance, fitter to accompany the virgins of Israel in their dances than to lead on the men of Israel in their battles."

The witnesses do not divide; they hear in the giant's scorn the very temper that dooms him. His pride can conceive of no danger from so slight a figure, and so he despises the instrument God has chosen precisely because it is despicable in his eyes. The commentators let the contempt run to its full length — the sneer at David's youth, ruddiness, and shepherd's sticks — knowing that the reader already sees what Goliath cannot: that the God of the armies of Israel delights to overturn the mighty by the mean, and that the giant's disdain for the boy is, in truth, disdain for the Lord who sends him.
43:
Goliath voices his scorn aloud — "Am I a dog, that thou comest to me with staves?" — and curses David by his god, and the witnesses attend to the insult, the curse, and a small point of translation. The Reformed Gill answers the giant's question with grim wit: "truly David did not think him much better, because of his impudence, impurity, and barking blasphemy against God and the armies of Israel," and notes the Targum's rendering, "Am I a despised dog?" The Wesleyan Clarke observes shrewdly that "it is very likely Goliath did not perceive the sling, which David might have kept coiled up within his hand" — so the giant, seeing only the staff, mocked an unarmed boy. Clarke reads "cursed David by his gods" as "prayed his gods to curse him," and sets the whole exchange in its ancient world: "this long parley is quite in the style of those times," like the Hindoo who cries "the goddess Kalee shall devour thee," or Homer's heroes who "vaunt and scold most contemptibly" before they fight.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds two touches. He pictures the giant, "seated, as was often the custom with these heavily-clad warriors when not actually engaged," rising and coming toward David to taunt him; and he notes that the Septuagint "missed the force of this plural of contempt" and altered the text to read "with staff and with stones." On the curse Ellicott presses a correction: "by his gods" should be rendered "by his God" — "no doubt the idolater here made use of the sacred Name, so dear to every believing Israelite, thus defying the Eternal of Hosts."

Here the witnesses show a small but real difference. Keil, at the previous verse, and Ellicott both take the giant to have cursed by Israel's own God, the sacred Name, making the blasphemy direct; Clarke reads it as Goliath invoking his heathen deities against David. The point is not idle: on Ellicott's reading the curse is itself a defiance of Jehovah, of a piece with the giant's whole affront to "the armies of the living God"; on Clarke's it is the futile imprecation of an idolater. Either way the commentators agree on the temper of the scene — a proud champion heaping abuse on a boy he takes for defenceless, blind alike to the hidden sling and to the God whose Name he flings about. The parley of insults, so like the taunts of the Iliad, is the last swelling of a pride about to be silenced by a single stone.
44:
Goliath crowns his taunts with a boast — "Come to me, and I will give thy flesh unto the fowls of the air and to the beasts of the field" — and the witnesses read it as the very height of a presumption about to be broken. The Reformed Gill notices that the giant "seems to have stood still, disdaining to take another step toward such a pitiful combatant," and so bids David come up to him; or perhaps, Gill suggests, because "David was light and nimble, and he heavy and unwieldy because of his bigness and the burden of armour," Goliath could not close the distance as fast as he wished "to destroy his adversary, of which he made no doubt." The Wesleyan Clarke gives the giant's intent in a line: "he intended, as soon as he could lay hold on him, to pull him to pieces."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the boast beside its Homeric echo, quoting Hector's defiance of Ajax: "if thy madness wait the lance of Hector, thou shalt meet thy fate; that giant corse, extended on the shore, shall largely feed the fowls with fat and gore." The parallel is exact — the same threat to leave an enemy's body for the carrion birds, the same swelling confidence before the duel.

The witnesses do not divide, and the material is spare, but they let the verse register the full arrogance of the giant's certainty. He does not doubt the outcome for a moment; he is already disposing of David's corpse, promising it to the fowls and beasts, as though the killing were a formality. The commentators mark the irony that the reader cannot miss: within a few verses it is the carcases of the Philistines, not of David, that will be given to the birds of the air, for David will fling the very same threat back at him and see it fulfilled. Goliath's boast is thus the last and loudest note of a pride that has ripened, through forty days of unanswered defiance, into a fatal security. The commentators hear in his words the settled conviction that no God stands behind the boy — and it is precisely that God, unreckoned with, who will turn the giant's threat upon his own head, and make his boast the epitaph of his whole army.
45:
David answers the giant not with a boast of his own but with the Name of God, and the witnesses read his reply as the theological heart of the whole chapter. The Wesleyan Clarke fixes the contrast in a sentence: "I come to thee with the name of Jehovah of hosts, the God of the armies of Israel. What Goliath expected from his arms, David expected from the ineffable name." Where the Philistine trusts in sword, spear, and shield, David trusts in the Lord of hosts whom the giant has defied.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the answer in full: "Thou comest to me with sword and javelin and lance; but I come to thee in the name of the Lord of Sabaoth, the God of the ranks of Israel, whom thou hast defied." Keil hears in it "bold, believing courage," the settled confidence of a man who has already, in his own mind, won the battle by reckoning with God rather than with the giant's brass.

The Puritan Matthew Henry unfolds the double title David invokes. "The name of God David relied on, as Goliath did on his sword and spear." He comes "in the name of the Lord," from whom he has his warrant; and that Lord is "the Lord of hosts, of all hosts, and therefore has power to do what he pleases," and "the God of the armies of Israel, and therefore has engaged and will employ his power for their protection, and against thee who hast impiously defied them." Henry sets the whole reliance under the Psalmist's word, "some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we will remember the name of the Lord our God" (compare Psalms 20:7). The Reformed Gill adds only that David spoke "in answer to the contempt and the threatening words" of the giant.

The witnesses do not divide; they converge with one voice on the meaning of the verse. David reframes the combat entirely: it is not youth against giant, nor sling against spear, but the Name of the living God against a man who has dared to defy him. The commentators dwell on the two-edged title — the Lord of all hosts, sovereign over every power, and the God of Israel's armies, covenanted to their defence — because in it lies the ground of David's certainty and the giant's doom. Goliath has weapons; David has the Name; and the whole point of the chapter, the witnesses agree, is that the Name is enough.
46:
David does not stop at faith but foretells the outcome in detail — "this day will the Lord deliver thee into mine hand, and I will smite thee and take thine head from thee" — and the witnesses read the words as prophecy grounded in inspiration. The Reformed Gill insists David could speak with such particularity only "by divine inspiration, by the impulse of the Spirit of God upon him; or otherwise he could not have expressed himself with such certainty, and have given the particulars of what he should do." The Wesleyan Clarke agrees that this was "a direct and circumstantial prophecy of what did take place."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the sweep of the prediction — not only the giant's head but "the carcases of the host of the Philistines" to the birds and beasts — and its avowed purpose: "that all the earth may know that there is a God in Israel." Ellicott stresses that David "reiterated to the Philistine, as he had done to Saul, his certainty of victory, but in the same breath says that the victory will be that God's whose name the Philistine had just been contemptuously using."

The Wesleyan Wesley weighs the closing phrase. "A God," he says, means "that God, the only true God, is for Israel, on Israel's side, and against you"; or else, "that Israel hath a God, a God indeed, one who is able to help them, and not such an impotent idol as you serve." The Puritan Matthew Henry reads David's whole aim as the honour of God: "he did not, like Goliath, seek his own honour, but the honour of God," not doubting that by this victory "all the world should be made to know that there is a God, and that the God of Israel is the one only living and true God."

The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as faith turned prophetic. David announces the giant's death, the rout of his army, and the carrion feast that will follow, with a confidence that the commentators can only explain by the Spirit's impulse. And they agree on the great end that governs the boldness: not David's fame, not even Israel's safety first of all, but that "all the earth may know that there is a God in Israel." The deliverance is prophesied so exactly, and its purpose named so plainly, that when it comes to pass the glory can be claimed by no arm and no name but the Lord's — the very Name the giant had just despised.
47:
David widens his prophecy to a doctrine — "all this assembly shall know that the Lord saveth not with sword and spear, for the battle is the Lord's" — and the witnesses read it as the lesson the whole scene is staged to teach. The Reformed Gill takes "this assembly" to be "the congregation of Israel and church of the living God," great part of which stood by "as spectators of this wonderful event." The Wesleyan Clarke hears in "the battle is the Lord's" the language of holy war: "it is the Lord's war; you are fighting against him and his religion, as the champion of your party; I am fighting for God, as the champion of his cause." Wesley unfolds the phrase "saveth not with sword and spear" as meaning "that he can save without these arms, and with the most contemptible weapons," and adds that "the events of war are wholly in his power," David speaking so confidently "because he was assured of it by a particular inspiration."

The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the same truth and its surprising instrument. David calls the gathered people not "this army" but "this assembly, or church, because they were now religiously attending the goings of their God and King"; and they "shall know that the Lord saveth not with sword and spear, but can, when he pleases, save without either and against both" (compare Psalms 46:9). Henry adds the striking image that David "addresses himself to this combat rather as a priest that was going to offer a sacrifice to the justice of God than as a soldier that was going to engage an enemy of his country."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott connects the verse to David's later songs. Though no ode survives from the day itself, "in many of the compositions attributed to him in the Psalter we find memories of this his first great triumph" — as in "I will not trust in my bow, neither shall my sword save me" (Psalms 44:6-8), and "there is no king saved by the multitude of an host; a mighty man is not delivered by much strength" (Psalms 33:16-20).

The witnesses do not divide; they hear one confession from all sides. The combat is arranged, in David's own words, to prove a doctrine before a watching church: that salvation is God's, not the sword's; that the battle belongs to the Lord; that he saves by the weakest means to make the lesson unmistakable. Henry's priestly image and Ellicott's echoes in the Psalter carry the same weight — David goes out not to display his skill but to vindicate the God who saves without sword or spear, so that the whole assembly may learn where deliverance truly lies.
48:
The combat is joined at last, and the witnesses set the two advances side by side. The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the contrast most vividly. The Philistine "advanced with a great deal of state and gravity; if he must encounter a pigmy, yet it shall be with the magnificence of a giant and a grandee" — "he arose, and came, and drew nigh, like a stalking mountain overlaid with brass and iron, to meet David." David, by contrast, "advanced with no less activity and cheerfulness, as one that aimed more to do execution than to make a figure: he hasted, and ran, being lightly clad, to meet the Philistine." Henry imagines "with what tenderness and compassion the Israelites saw such a pleasing youth throwing himself into the mouth of destruction, but he knew whom he had believed and for whom he acted."

The Wesleyan commentators strike the same note. Wesley pictures the giant drawing nigh "like a stalking mountain," and David running, "so far was he from fear." Clarke marks it soberly as the end of the parley: "the Philistine came forward to meet David, and David on his part ran forward to meet the Philistine." The Reformed Gill supposes the giant "arose, or prepared for the encounter, in all probability in great wrath and fury at hearing what David said, and which hastened him to it."

The witnesses do not divide; they read the double advance as the visible image of the two trusts that have been set against each other throughout the chapter. The giant comes on slowly, weighted with the brass in which he trusts, magnificent and deliberate; David runs, unarmoured and eager, because his confidence is not in what he carries but in the God for whom he acts. Henry's phrase "he knew whom he had believed" is the key the commentators turn: David's haste is not recklessness but faith, the boldness of a man already sure of the outcome. The Israelites watch in dread, seeing only a boy rushing upon a mountain of a man; but the reader, the witnesses insist, is meant to see the true proportions reversed — the ponderous giant lumbering to his fall, and the running shepherd carrying, in an unseen sling, the judgment of the living God.
49:
The stone flies, sinks into Goliath's forehead, and the giant falls on his face — and the witnesses dwell on the one exposed point and the deadly skill that found it. The Reformed Gill follows the action plainly: David "put his hand in his bag," took a stone, slung it, and smote the Philistine. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the blow: the stone "hit the Philistine on his temples, so that the stone entered them, and Goliath fell upon his face to the ground."

The Wesleyan Clarke explains why the forehead lay open: "except his face, Goliath was everywhere covered over with strong armour. Either he had no beaver to his helmet, or it was lifted up." Clarke then defends the force of the blow at length from Diodorus Siculus, who reports that the Baleares slung stones so powerfully that "they break in pieces the shields, helmets, and every species of armour," and were "such exact marksmen that they scarcely ever miss." Ellicott adds that the Septuagint, unable to credit an unprotected forehead, inserted "through the helm," but "the head-pieces of the armour then do not appear to have possessed visors"; the face was covered by the great shield borne before him (see v.7), which the giant, "utterly despising his youthful antagonist, advanced without using." Ellicott recalls how the Benjamites of old could "sling stones at an hair's breadth and not miss" (compare Judges 20:16).

The Wesleyan Wesley and the Puritan Matthew Henry together read providence into the exposed brow. Wesley supposes "the proud giant had lifted up that part of his helmet which covered his forehead, in contempt of David, and by the singular direction of providence." Henry marvels that Goliath, who knew there were famous slingers in Israel, was "either so forgetful or presumptuous as to go with the beaver of his helmet open," and to that one spot "not so much David's art as God's providence directed the stone."

The witnesses do not divide; they hold skill and providence together. David's marksmanship is real, honed in the sheepfold and matched by the deadly slingers of antiquity; yet the one gap in all that brass, and the stone's unerring flight to it, they ascribe to the hand of God. The giant's own contempt — the lifted visor, the shield left with its bearer — becomes the instrument of his fall, so that his pride literally uncovers the target. The commentators let the moment carry the chapter's whole argument: the Lord saves not with sword and spear, but guides a shepherd's stone to the one unguarded point of a mountain of armour.
50:
The narrator pauses to sum up the wonder — "so David prevailed over the Philistine with a sling and with a stone, but there was no sword in the hand of David" — and the witnesses read the summary as the verse's whole point. The Reformed Gill notes that David prevailed "with them only, without any other warlike weapon." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as "a remark by the historian with reference to the result of the conflict": "thus was David stronger than the Philistine, with a sling and stone, and smote the Philistine and slew him without a sword in his hand" — the detail of cutting off the head with Goliath's own sword being reserved for the next verse.

Ellicott dwells on how narrow the margin had been, precisely to magnify the deliverance. The story "dwells, and with good reason, on the extraordinary valour and skill of the young champion." Had his heart for one instant failed him "as, indeed, it well might; had he not possessed a confidence which nothing could shake in an unseen Helper; or had his skill as a marksman failed him in the slightest degree, the Philistine with one blow would have laid David lifeless at his feet," or the boy must have saved himself by flight; and in either case "the single combat upon which Israel had staked so much would have gone against the chosen people."

The witnesses do not divide; the material is spare, but they let the narrator's own emphasis govern their reading. The point is not merely that David won, but how — "with a sling and with a stone," and pointedly "no sword in the hand of David." The commentators hear in that repeated stress the fulfilment of David's own confession that "the Lord saveth not with sword and spear." The mightiest warrior of the Philistines, armoured head to foot and armed with a weaver's-beam spear, is overcome by an unarmoured boy wielding a shepherd's sling; and the historian underlines the absence of a sword in David's hand so that no reader can mistake where the strength lay. Ellicott's meditation on how easily it might have gone otherwise serves the same end: the very slenderness of the human means throws the whole weight of the victory upon the God whose battle it was, and whose glory the empty-handed triumph was designed to proclaim.
51:
David runs to the fallen giant, dispatches him with his own sword, and cuts off his head, and the Philistines flee — and the witnesses read the scene as poetic justice and the turning of the battle. The Reformed Gill pictures David standing upon the carcass and trampling it, "in just contempt of him who had defied, reproached, and despised the armies of Israel." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow the action: David "took his sword, drew it out of the sheath, and slew him, and cut off his head therewith," and the Philistines, seeing their champion dead, fled — pursued "to the valley and to the gates of Ekron," the wounded falling by the way to Shaaraim (compare Joshua 15:36).

The Wesleyan Wesley draws a physical inference from the deed: "hence it appears that David was not a little man, as many fancy, but a man of considerable bulk and strength, because he was able to manage a giant's sword." The stone, he explains, "threw him down to the earth and bereaved him of sense and motion, but there remained some life in him, which the sword took away." And he sounds the note of justice: "God is greatly glorified when his proud enemies are cut off with their own sword."

The Puritan Matthew Henry presses the same theme. David "drew Goliath's own sword, a two-handed weapon for David, and with it cut off his head. What need had David to take a sword of his own? his enemy's sword shall serve his purpose." "God is greatly glorified," Henry adds, "when his proud enemies are cut off with their own sword, and he makes their own tongues to fall upon them" (compare Psalms 64:8). The Wesleyan Clarke and the grammatical-historical Ellicott both mark the Philistines' collapse: they were "panic-struck," and, seeing the shepherd boy with the head of their champion in his hand, Israel "felt that the old power had come back to them, that once more their Invisible King was with them," and charged their dismayed foes with "an irresistible shout."

The witnesses do not divide; they read the beheading as the seal of a victory already won by the stone, and as a parable of God's dealing with the proud. The giant who boasted of leaving David's flesh for the birds is slain with his own blade, his head struck off with the very sword he trusted; and the army that would never have kept his surrender-terms breaks and runs at his fall. The commentators let the reversal stand complete: the reproach of forty days is lifted in a moment, and the sword drawn to shame Israel becomes the instrument of Israel's triumph.
52:
The rout becomes a pursuit, and the witnesses attend to the geography and the sudden reversal of Israel's fortunes. The Reformed Gill marks the change in the men who had cowered: "the men of Israel and of Judah arose from their encampment and entrenchment," rising up to give chase to the enemy they had fled from an hour before. The grammatical-historical Ellicott does the close topographical work. He notes that the Hebrew reads not "the valley" but "a valley" — "there is no article" — which "at once suggests that the valley here spoken of so indefinitely was not that well-known ravine which divided the two armies," across which the Philistines had never come. Ellicott records that the Septuagint reads "to Gath" instead of "to a valley," and thinks the Greek translators "probably had before them the true text: Gath, instead of gai, a valley." Shaaraim he identifies as a town in the lowlands of Judah (see Joshua 15:36), its name perhaps preserved in the modern Kefr Zakariya, though the Septuagint takes it not as a city but as "the way of the gates," the space between the outer and inner gates of Ekron.

The witnesses do not divide; the material is largely descriptive, but they let the verse register the completeness of the turn. The pursuit runs "to the gates of Ekron," deep into Philistine territory, and the road is strewn with the wounded of the fleeing host — a scene the exact reverse of the panic that had gripped Israel while Goliath lived. The commentators' careful sorting of "valley" and "Gath," of Shaaraim as town or gateway, serves the larger picture: the victory of one shepherd has unloosed a general flight, and the armies that dared not cross the ravine now sweep the enemy back to their own cities. Where the chapter opened with the Philistines pressing into the land of Judah and encamping on its hills, it now shows them driven headlong to Ekron and Gath, the border of blood recrossed in the opposite direction. The single stone has become a national deliverance, and the men of Israel and Judah, so lately dismayed, rise with a shout to gather in what David's faith has won.
53:
The chase ends and the plunder begins, and the witnesses read the spoiling of the Philistine camp as the full harvest of David's victory. The Reformed Gill pictures the pursuit breaking off once "the remainder that escaped had got into their fortified cities"; the children of Israel then "returned from chasing after the Philistines" and fell to gathering the abandoned camp. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the scene plainly: "after returning from the pursuit of the flying foe, the Israelites plundered the camp of the Philistines," noting that the verb for their hot pursuit is the same used of Laban chasing Jacob (compare Genesis 31:36).

The Puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the aftermath, marks the completeness of the reversal: "in their return from the chase they seized all the baggage, plundered the tents, and enriched themselves with the spoil." The army that had trembled before one man now strips the camp of the many.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse is spare, but they let it carry the irony that runs through the chapter's close. Goliath had proposed that the losing nation should serve the winner; the Philistines, when he fell, kept no such bargain but fled — and the outcome was worse for them than servitude, for their camp was left to be pillaged and their tents given over to the spoilers. The commentators see in the plunder the tangible sign of a deliverance that was total: not merely the death of a champion, but the flight of an army and the enriching of the people who had been on the brink of subjection. The God who saves not with sword and spear has not only lifted Israel's reproach but filled their hands with the enemy's goods. Keil's small philological note, linking the hot pursuit to the language of Genesis, and Henry's tally of baggage seized and tents plundered, serve the same end: to show the completeness of the turn from panic to plunder. What began with a boy's sling ends with a nation gathering spoil, and the witnesses let the plundered tents stand as the plain, homely proof that the battle was indeed the Lord's, and that those who honour him are not the poorer for it. The camp that had held Israel in dread for forty days is emptied into Israel's hands in an afternoon, and the reversal is left to speak for itself.
54:
David carries Goliath's head to Jerusalem and lays up the giant's armour, and the witnesses take up two small puzzles: why Jerusalem, and what "his tent" means. On the first, the Reformed Gill supposes David carried the head "in triumph" through various cities, and brought it to Jerusalem — though "not a royal city, nor wholly in the hands of the Israelites" — most likely "to the terror of the Jebusites, who held the strong-hold of Zion." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott both answer the apparent anachronism: there is no real difficulty, for "the assertion that Jerusalem was not yet in the possession of the Israelites rests upon a confusion between the citadel of Jebus upon Zion, still held by the Jebusites, and the city of Jerusalem, in which Israelites had long dwelt" (compare Joshua 15:63; Judges 1:21). Ellicott calls it "this place of arms, naturally selected for the home of the famous trophy, being the nearest stronghold to the scene of the victory."

On "his tent" the commentators divide gently. Keil takes the word for an "antiquated term for a dwelling-place," meaning David's own house at Bethlehem, to which he returned with the booty; there is no contradiction with the sword being later kept at Nob (compare 1 Samuel 21:9), since David need not have kept it there permanently. Ellicott reports the same reading but records Abarbanel's alternative "with great probability": that "in his tent" means "the tabernacle of Jehovah," so termed pointedly because David in later days "pitched it" in his own city (compare 2 Samuel 6:17) — the New Testament itself calling the sacred tent "the tabernacle of David" (see Acts 15:16). The Wesleyan Clarke adds only that the verse, with those around it, is among the passages the Septuagint omits.

The debate is fine, not fierce: whether "his tent" is David's Bethlehem dwelling or the tabernacle of the Lord. What the witnesses agree on is the meaning of the act. The head borne to Jerusalem is a trophy of terror to the enemies still lodged on Zion; the armour laid up, and the sword afterward preserved behind the ephod, are memorials of a victory that belonged to God. Whether the young hero stored his spoils at home or dedicated them to the sanctuary, the commentators see the same instinct — that the tokens of so signal a deliverance should be kept as a standing witness that the Lord saves his people.
55:
Saul, watching David go out, asks Abner whose son the youth is, and Abner cannot tell — and the witnesses face here the chapter's gravest difficulty of harmony. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the whole exchange as "a supplementary remark," the verbs to be rendered as pluperfects: while David was fighting, Saul had asked Abner about him. Keil insists the question "does not presuppose an actual want of acquaintance with the person of David and the name of his father," but concerns his family and standing.

Ellicott states the problem frankly: in the previous chapter David is chosen to play before the king, relieves him, and is appointed his armour-bearer (compare 1 Samuel 16:21-23), yet here "this same David, at the time of his great exploit, was apparently unknown to the king and to Abner." He notes that the Septuagint, "fully conscious of the difficulty, determined to solve it by boldly cutting the knot," expunging these four verses, the first five of the next chapter, and the whole section from v.12 to v.31. Ellicott then canvasses the explanations: that Saul's mental state left him unable to recognise the young musician; that time had elapsed and David had grown out of the king's memory; or that Saul's inquiry sought not David's identity but his father's position, in view of the reward promised (v.25). His own "real solution" is that the historical books were compiled "from well-authenticated traditions," and that two overlapping narratives of David's early days were here set side by side — one preserved in a school of prophetic music, dwelling on the harp that soothed the king, the other concerned to show "that the foundation stone of David's brilliant life was intense faith in the Jehovah of Israel."

The Wesleyan Wesley and the Reformed Gill take the simpler road. Wesley notes that "David had been some considerable time dismissed from Saul's court, and was returned home," so it is "not strange if Saul for the present had forgot David"; besides, "the distemper of Saul's mind might make him forgetful," and David "might be now much changed in his countenance and his habit." As for Abner, "his employment was generally in the camp when David was at the court," so he had taken little notice of one so far beneath him.

The witnesses thus divide over method, not over faith in the text. Ellicott resolves the tension by the theory of two documents woven together; Wesley and Gill resolve it by Saul's distemper, David's absence, and Abner's unfamiliarity. Both refuse the Septuagint's knife. All agree that the question, whatever puzzles it raises, is really about the family of the champion who is about to be honoured, and that the difficulty yields to patient reading rather than to excision.
56:
Saul renews his question — "inquire thou whose son the stripling is" — and the witnesses read the king's persistence as evidence that his interest is fixed on David's family rather than merely his name. The Reformed Gill observes that "still the question is the same," Saul "being very desirous of knowing of what family he was, for the reason before given" — the reward that awaited the victor's house. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the surrounding action: when David returned from the slaughter of the Philistine, Abner — "who probably went as commander to meet the brave hero and congratulate him upon his victory" — brought him to Saul, and the king addressed the same question to David himself, "who immediately gave him the information he desired."

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse is brief, but they let the repeated inquiry reinforce the reading offered at v.55. That Saul asks twice, and then puts the question a third time to David in person, tells against the notion that he is simply ignorant of who the young man is; a king who had employed David as musician and armour-bearer would hardly need to be told his name three times over. Rather, the commentators suggest, the persistence points to a settled purpose: Saul means to learn the standing of the family he has pledged to enrich and ennoble, and will not let the matter drop until he has it from the hero's own mouth. Keil's picture of Abner going out to congratulate the victor and lead him before the king fills in the honour of the moment — the captain of the host escorting the shepherd, the head of the giant in his hand, to the royal presence. The word "stripling" keeps the wonder before the reader: it is a mere youth, a slight figure, whom the king is so anxious to know and to reward. The verse thus holds together the two things the chapter has kept in tension — the smallness of the instrument and the greatness of what God has wrought through him — as Saul, twice unsatisfied, presses to discover the lineage of the boy who has done what his whole army dared not.
57:
David returns from the slaughter, and Abner brings him before Saul with the giant's head still in his hand — and the witnesses read the brief verse as the triumphal presentation of the new champion. The Reformed Gill fills in the scene: David came "carrying his head in triumph, and no doubt accompanied with the acclamations of the people." The moment gathers up all that has gone before: the shepherd who came to the camp with loaves and cheeses now stands before the king bearing the head of the Philistine who had defied the armies of the living God.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, taking this verse with the one before, picture Abner meeting the returning hero to congratulate him and leading him to Saul — the captain of the host himself escorting the youth into the royal presence. The Puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the introduction, notes that Saul, "having been for some time absent from him, and being melancholy and mindless, had forgotten him," and so "as if he had never seen him before, he asked whose son he was"; Abner, "a stranger to him, brought him to Saul."

The witnesses do not divide, and the material is spare, but they let the tableau speak. Every eye is on the boy and the grim trophy he carries. The head of the giant in David's hand is the visible proof that the reproach is lifted, the army delivered, the living God vindicated — and it is that proof which Abner presents and Saul receives. The commentators feel the drama of the contrast: the towering champion who filled the valley with his voice for forty days is reduced to a severed head borne by a stripling, while the king who dared not fight now looks upon the one who did. Henry's note that David is introduced "with much greater advantages than before" catches the turn: the young musician who once entered the court to calm the king with his harp now enters it as the deliverer of Israel, the whole nation's acclamation behind him. The verse sets him, head of the enemy in hand, at the threshold of the public life to which his victory has raised him — and prepares the modest words with which, in the next verse, he will name himself before the king.
58:
Saul asks the young victor whose son he is, and David answers simply, "I am the son of thy servant Jesse the Bethlehemite" — and the witnesses close the chapter on both the modesty of the reply and the sharpest form of its textual difficulty. The Reformed Gill keeps to the reading he has held throughout: the question "did not necessarily imply ignorance of his person, but of his family." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that David plainly "said more than is here communicated," since the opening of the next chapter presupposes "a protracted conversation between Saul and David" (compare 1 Samuel 18:1); the rest was not recorded, they suggest, "because it was not followed by any lasting results either for Jesse or David."

Here the Wesleyan Clarke presses the difficulty to its limit and offers his solution. That Saul "should not know David, with whom he had treated a little before, and even armed him for the combat," he confesses "exceedingly strange," and finds Bishop Warburton's attempts to rectify the chronology unsatisfying. He therefore lays out at length the argument of Pilkington and Kennicott that the whole section from v.12 to v.31, with these closing verses, is "an interpolation of some rabbinical writer," absent from the Vatican Septuagint; Kennicott urges that the Alexandrian manuscript, beginning its twelfth verse with the very words that open v.32, betrays a scribe copying from a text that lacked the intervening verses, and that Josephus's account is free of "half the improbabilities" now found in the Hebrew.

The debate joined at v.12 thus reaches its climax: Clarke, with Kennicott and Pilkington, doubts the passage as a late insertion; Ellicott and Keil defend it as genuine, the fruit of two contemporary documents honestly combined. Clarke ends, characteristically, on the reality of giants themselves, recalling brothers of his own township "seven feet six inches high," one Charles Byrne "eight feet six," and, at the other extreme, the perfectly formed Count Boruwlaski of "about thirty-six inches" — man being "the only creature in whom the extremes of minuteness and magnitude are so apparent."

The witnesses do not finally divide over the substance of the story, only over the integrity of the text. And beneath the criticism they let David's own word stand as the note on which the chapter ends: no boast, no claim of the promised reward, but the plain confession of a servant's son from Bethlehem — the humility that has marked the shepherd from the first, unchanged in the hour of his greatest triumph.
